fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened a savings account with your bank in 2016 after seeing your promotional material that described the interest rate as 'guaranteed at 2.5% for the life of the account'. I now understand that the rate was only fixed for 12 months and then reverted to a variable rate, which has been as low as 0.1%. I never received any communication clarifying that 'guaranteed' only meant the introductory period. I feel I was misled by your advertising and have lost out on interest I could have earned elsewhere over the past eight years. I am 78 years old and rely on my savings income to supplement my state pension, so this has caused me real financial hardship. I would like compensation for the difference in interest I should have received. I raised this with your branch last week and they said the complaint might be 'too old' but didn't explain what that meant.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious issues that require careful human review before any response is sent to the customer.

**Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:**

1. **Misleading advertising / interest rate presentation:** The retrieved provisions (BCOBS 2.2.5R area and BCOBS 2 Annex 2G) address how interest rates on savings accounts must be presented — they must not give the impression a rate is available when it is unlikely to be obtained, and where introductory or promotional rates apply, the summary box should include details of this. BCOBS 2.2A.2G (Annex 2) specifically indicates that where a bonus or conditional rate applies, this must be clearly distinguished. If the promotional material described the rate as 'guaranteed for the life of the account' without making clear it was only an introductory rate, this is potentially a misleading communication under the BCOBS fair, clear and not misleading standard. However, the retrieved provisions do not give me the full text of the binding rule (e.g. the core BCOBS 2.2.1R or equivalent) — I can see guidance and annexes but not the primary rule in full. I am therefore flagging insufficient context on the precise binding rule basis.

2. **Notification obligations:** BCOBS 4 provisions retrieved (bcobs4s1) indicate that firms should notify customers of material changes to interest rates and of the expiry of introductory/promotional rates. If no such notification was provided when the rate reverted after 12 months, this may represent a failure of the firm's ongoing disclosure obligations. These are framed as guidance ('should') in the retrieved text, not as hard rules, so the handler should check whether there is an underlying binding rule in BCOBS 4 that these provisions support.

3. **Time/limitation concerns:** The branch mentioned the complaint might be 'too old'. The retrieved provisions do not include DISP time-limit rules, so I cannot advise on the precise limitation position from the retrieved context alone. This must be checked against DISP 1.6 and DISP 2.8 (time limits for referring to FOS) by the handler or compliance team. Do not make any representation to the customer about time limits until this is confirmed.

4. **Customer vulnerability:** The customer is 78 years old, relies on savings income to supplement their state pension, and has suffered what they describe as real financial hardship over eight years. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The response must be handled with appropriate care and the firm's vulnerable customer policies should be applied.

5. **Compensation claim:** The customer is seeking compensation for the interest differential over eight years. This is a financial redress question that cannot be assessed or committed to without a full investigation. Do not quantify or promise any redress in the customer letter.

6. **Assumptions to confirm before sending the draft below:**
 - Confirm whether the original promotional material has been retrieved and what it actually said.
 - Confirm whether any notifications of rate change or introductory rate expiry were sent to this customer.
 - Confirm the complaint receipt date and whether DISP time-limit rules affect the firm's obligations or the customer's FOS referral rights.
 - Confirm whether the account was opened before or after relevant BCOBS provisions came into force (2016 opening — check applicable version of BCOBS at that date).
 - Apply the firm's vulnerable customer policy given the customer's age and financial reliance on savings income.

The draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms it is being investigated, and preserves the customer's rights — but does NOT admit liability, quantify redress, or make representations about time limits. It must be reviewed by a human before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us about your savings account and the concerns you have raised about the way the interest rate was described when you opened it in 2016. I am sorry to hear that this situation has caused you financial hardship, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have told us that the promotional material you saw described the interest rate as 'guaranteed at 2.5% for the life of the account', and that you were not made aware that this rate was only fixed for an introductory period before reverting to a variable rate. You have also told us that you did not receive any communication from us clarifying this when the rate changed. We understand that, as someone who relies on your savings income, this matters greatly to you.

We are required to communicate with our customers in a way that is fair, clear and not misleading, and where an introductory or promotional rate applies to a savings account, information about how that rate works — including when it ends — should be made clear. We take these obligations seriously and we will investigate your complaint fully, including reviewing the promotional material in question and our records of any communications sent to you about changes to your interest rate.

We will write to you again with our findings and our final response. We aim to do this as promptly as possible, and we will keep you informed of our progress.

Regarding the comment made at our branch that your complaint might be 'too old': we understand this may have been confusing and we apologise that it was not explained to you at the time. We will address this point as part of our investigation and will explain the position clearly in our final response.

If, once you have received our final response, you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, which is a free and independent service for consumers. We will provide you with full details of how to do this when we send our final response.

Thank you again for bringing this to our attention. We will be in touch shortly.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

Guidance on presentation of interest-rate information in savings account summary boxes, including where introductory or promotional rates apply — relevant to the adequacy of disclosure at account opening.

Indicates that firms should notify banking customers of material changes to interest rates and of the expiry of introductory or promotional rates — relevant to whether the firm met its ongoing disclosure obligations when the rate reverted.

The underlying binding rule on communications with banking customers to which the guidance in BCOBS 4 refers — relevant as the binding basis for the firm's disclosure obligations, though the full text was not retrieved.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a peer-to-peer loan of £15,000 through LendConnect Platform in March this year. Within 10 days of receiving the agreement, I sent an email to the address specified in the contract stating I wished to withdraw from the agreement. I received an automated acknowledgement confirming receipt of my email. However, the platform then charged me a £250 'early exit administration fee' on top of the accrued interest, claiming this was standard for withdrawals. When I queried this, I was told it was in the terms. I also want to note that when I originally signed up to the platform, I was never given any information about how to make a complaint or about the Financial Ombudsman Service — not at the point of sale, not on their website, and not in the contract terms. I only found out about my right to complain by searching online. I believe the exit fee is unlawful and I want it refunded, and I'm unhappy that I was never told about my complaint rights.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues, both of which appear well-founded on the retrieved provisions.

**Issue 1: The £250 'early exit administration fee'**

CONC 11.2.3R (as reflected in the retrieved chunk from conc11s2.md) sets out what a borrower must and must not pay upon exercising the right of withdrawal from a P2P agreement. The borrower must repay the credit provided and accrued interest at the contractual rate, but is expressly not liable to pay any compensation, fees or charges — except any non-returnable charges paid by the lender or firm to a public administrative body. A £250 'early exit administration fee' does not fall within that narrow exception and therefore appears to be prohibited under CONC 11.2.3R. CONC 11.2.4R reinforces this by requiring that the P2P agreement itself must not provide for any other obligations of the borrower in connection with exercising the withdrawal right. On the face of it, charging this fee — and including a term purporting to authorise it — appears to breach both rules. The customer's position that the fee is unlawful appears well-founded.

Assumption to confirm: The customer states the email was sent within 10 days of receiving the agreement. You should verify the exact date the agreement was received and the date the withdrawal email was sent, to confirm the withdrawal was exercised within the applicable window. The retrieved provisions do not specify the withdrawal period length — this is a gap in the retrieved context. Flag this as insufficient context and route to human review before committing to a position on whether the withdrawal was validly exercised in time.

**Issue 2: Failure to provide complaints/FOS information**

COBS 5.3 Annex 1 (19) (retrieved chunk cobs5s3.md) requires that customers be given information about how to complain to the firm and whether complaints may be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service, including methods for access. The customer states this information was absent at point of sale, on the website, and in the contract terms. If accurate, this appears to be a breach of that disclosure requirement.

**Recommended action:**
- Uphold both aspects of the complaint in principle, subject to confirming the withdrawal timeline.
- Recommend refund of the £250 fee, but do not commit to a specific remedy amount in the customer letter — flag for human review given the financial redress element.
- The customer draft below acknowledges both issues, confirms the regulatory position in plain English, and sets out referral rights to FOS.

**Human review is required** because: (1) the complaint involves financial redress (refund of £250 fee); (2) the withdrawal period length is not confirmed by the retrieved provisions, creating an assumption that must be verified; (3) the firm (LendConnect) may have legal exposure on both counts and a senior reviewer should sign off before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for contacting us about your experience with LendConnect Platform. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have encountered, and I want to assure you that we have taken both of your concerns seriously.

**Your withdrawal and the administration fee**

When a borrower exercises the right to withdraw from a peer-to-peer lending agreement, the rules that apply to this type of agreement are clear about what can and cannot be charged. A borrower who withdraws is required to repay the credit received and any interest that has accrued at the rate set out in the agreement. However, a borrower is not liable to pay any compensation, fees or other charges in connection with exercising that withdrawal right — other than in very limited circumstances involving charges paid to a public body, which do not apply here.

Furthermore, the terms of a peer-to-peer agreement must not impose any additional obligations on a borrower as a result of exercising the right to withdraw.

On the basis of what you have described, the £250 'early exit administration fee' that was charged to you appears to fall outside what is permitted. We are reviewing this matter and will write to you again with our findings and next steps. [NOTE FOR HANDLER: Do not confirm refund in this letter until human review is complete and the withdrawal timeline is verified.]

**Information about complaints and the Financial Ombudsman Service**

You have also raised a concern that you were never provided with information about how to make a complaint or about the Financial Ombudsman Service. Firms are required to give customers this information — including how to complain and how to access the Financial Ombudsman Service — and we take this obligation seriously. We are sorry that you had to find this information yourself, and we are looking into this aspect of your complaint as well.

**What happens next**

We will complete our review and send you a full response as soon as possible. If you remain dissatisfied with our response once you receive it, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about how to do this in our full response.

Thank you again for bringing these matters to our attention.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Firm name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Sets out that a borrower exercising the right of withdrawal from a P2P agreement is not liable to pay compensation, fees or charges (beyond accrued interest and credit), making the £250 administration fee appear impermissible.

Requires that a P2P agreement must not provide for any other obligations of the borrower in connection with exercising the withdrawal right, meaning a contractual term purporting to authorise the fee would itself be non-compliant.

Requires firms to provide customers with information about how to complain and how to access the Financial Ombudsman Service; the customer's account that this was never provided suggests a potential breach.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am a 72-year-old retired teacher with limited investment experience. Last month I opened an execution-only account with your firm and attempted to purchase a complex leveraged ETF product. I recall being asked some questions about my knowledge and experience, and I told your adviser that I had never traded derivatives or leveraged products before. Despite this, I was not given any warning that the product might be inappropriate for me — the transaction simply went through. The product has since lost 40% of its value. I tried to call your complaints line to raise this issue but the only number listed on your website is an 0871 number, which cost me over £3 on hold before I gave up and wrote this letter instead. I believe your firm failed in its duty to warn me that this product was not appropriate given my lack of experience, and I also believe it is unacceptable that your complaints telephone line uses a revenue-sharing number that discourages customers from complaining.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two substantive issues: (1) alleged failure to provide an appropriateness warning for a leveraged/complex product on an execution-only basis, and (2) use of an 0871 revenue-sharing telephone number for the complaints line.

IMPORTANT: The retrieved provisions do not cover either of these issues adequately. The retrieved chunks relate to: (a) risk warning wording requirements for CFD/restricted options marketing materials (COBS 22); (b) financial promotion risk warnings for non-mass market investments and cryptoassets (COBS 4); and (c) endowment mortgage complaint assessment guidance (DISP App 1.1). None of these provisions address: the appropriateness assessment rules for execution-only transactions in complex/leveraged products (which would typically be found in COBS 10 or COBS 10A); the requirement to issue an appropriateness warning where a client lacks relevant knowledge/experience; or the prohibition on using revenue-sharing (non-geographic) telephone numbers for complaints handling (which would typically be found in DISP 1.3 or related rules).

Given this, I am setting insufficient_context to true and flagging for mandatory human review. I have drafted a holding acknowledgement letter only — it does not make any regulatory commitments on the substance of either issue, and does not admit liability or quantify redress. Before a substantive response is sent, the handler must:
1. Retrieve and apply the correct COBS 10/10A appropriateness rules and any related execution-only provisions.
2. Retrieve and apply the DISP rules on complaints telephone numbers (non-geographic number prohibition).
3. Consider the customer's age (72), retirement status, and stated limited investment experience as potential vulnerability indicators — a senior manager or specialist should review.
4. Note that a 40% loss on a leveraged product is a potential redress situation requiring careful assessment.
5. Confirm whether the complaint was received within any relevant time limits and whether any internal deadlines are at risk.
6. Confirm whether the 0871 number issue is a live systemic problem requiring separate regulatory notification.

The customer draft below is a holding acknowledgement only and should be safe to send pending full investigation, but human review is still required before dispatch given the vulnerability indicators and potential redress exposure.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear about the concerns you have raised, and we want to assure you that we take them seriously.

We have received your complaint, which covers two matters: first, whether you were given an appropriate warning before your leveraged ETF transaction was completed given the investment experience you described; and second, your experience trying to reach our complaints line using the telephone number listed on our website.

We are now investigating both issues carefully. We will write to you again with our findings and our response to your complaint as soon as we are able to do so.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us. We would also like to apologise for any inconvenience caused by the difficulty you experienced in reaching us by telephone.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Firm Name]
[Date]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I have a regulated mortgage with your bank and fell into arrears three months ago after being made redundant. I agreed a repayment arrangement with your collections team to pay £850 per month, which I have stuck to without fail. Despite this, I have been charged a £75 monthly arrears management fee each of those three months. When I called to query this, I was told it was 'standard policy' and no one could explain how the charge was calculated or what additional administration it covers. I also asked for a breakdown showing the charge doesn't include things like your general overheads or regulatory costs, but was told that information isn't available. On top of that, when I look at my statements, it appears my payments are being applied to these charges first rather than reducing the shortfall itself, which means my arrears balance is actually growing. I am on Universal Credit and struggling to keep up as it is. I want these charges refunded and a proper explanation of how my payments are being allocated.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns under MCOB 12 and MCOB 13 that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent. Here is the regulatory position based on the retrieved provisions:

**1. Legitimacy of the arrears management charges (MCOB 12.4.4R)**
MCOB 12.4.4R sets out what a firm must NOT take into account when calculating payment shortfall charges. Specifically, it prohibits including: funding or capital costs; general bank charges not incurred as a result of the payment shortfall; unrecovered fees; advertising costs; regulatory fines; financial reporting costs (unless objectively justifiable and solely related to arrears management); and executive staff costs (unless objectively justifiable and relating to day-to-day management of customers in payment shortfall). The customer's complaint that no one can explain how the £75 charge is calculated, and that a breakdown excluding general overheads and regulatory costs is unavailable, is a direct red flag against compliance with MCOB 12.4.4R. You must investigate whether the £75 charge has been calculated in accordance with this rule. If it includes any of the prohibited cost categories, the charges are unlawful and must be refunded.

**2. Transparency and the 'standard policy' response**
MCOB 12.4.2G indicates that firms may calculate the same level of charges for all customers in payment shortfall rather than on an individual basis — but this does not remove the obligation under MCOB 12.4.4R to ensure the charge reflects only permissible costs. The fact that collections staff could not explain the basis of the charge or provide a breakdown is a serious concern. You should escalate to whoever owns the arrears charge methodology to obtain the cost justification.

**3. Payment allocation — arrears growing despite compliance with arrangement**
The customer states payments are being applied to charges first, causing the arrears balance to grow. The retrieved provisions do not include a specific rule on payment allocation order, so I cannot make a definitive regulatory statement on this point from the retrieved context alone. However, this is a material concern that could interact with the excessive charges rules and the customer's interests. This must be reviewed by a senior handler or compliance team. Flag `insufficient_context` is set to true in part because of this gap.

**4. Information provision obligations (MCOB 13.4.1R and MCOB 13.5.1R)**
MCOB 13.4.1R requires that within 15 business days of becoming aware of arrears, the firm must provide the customer with: the MoneyHelper 'Problems paying your mortgage' information sheet; a list of missed/partial payments; the total payment shortfall; charges incurred; total outstanding debt (excluding redemption charges); and an indication of likely future charges. You should confirm whether this was done within the required timeframe three months ago. MCOB 13.5.1R also requires at least quarterly written statements showing payments due, actual shortfall, debt, and charges incurred. Check whether these have been issued.

**5. Vulnerability**
The customer is on Universal Credit, recently made redundant, and is struggling financially. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. All communications and decisions must be handled with appropriate care.

**Recommended actions before responding:**
- Obtain the cost methodology underpinning the £75 monthly arrears management charge and verify it complies with MCOB 12.4.4R.
- Confirm whether MCOB 13.4.1R disclosure was made within 15 business days of the account falling into arrears.
- Confirm whether quarterly statements under MCOB 13.5.1R have been issued.
- Investigate the payment allocation methodology and whether it is causing the arrears to grow.
- Do not commit to a refund in the customer letter at this stage — the draft below acknowledges the complaint and sets out next steps, but does not admit liability or promise a specific remedy. A financial decision on refund must be made by a human reviewer once the investigation is complete.
- Given vulnerability, consider whether any additional support (e.g. referral to debt advice) should be offered.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below does not include the FOS referral time-limit notice or the prescribed FOS wording because it is not a final response — it is an acknowledgement/holding response pending investigation. If this letter is intended to serve as a final response, it must be substantially revised and will need the FOS referral rights and time limits added, supported by the applicable DISP provisions (not retrieved here).
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have been facing following your redundancy, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously.

You have raised a number of important issues, and I want to be clear that we are treating this as a formal complaint. Specifically, you have raised concerns about:

- The monthly arrears management charges applied to your account over the past three months;
- The lack of explanation you received when you asked how those charges are calculated;
- The way your payments are being allocated, and whether this is causing your arrears balance to increase; and
- Your request for a refund of the charges.

We are required by our regulator to ensure that any charges applied to your account as a result of your payment shortfall are based only on the additional administration costs we have actually incurred as a direct result of your arrears. There are specific categories of cost — including general overheads, regulatory costs, and certain other items — that we are not permitted to include in those charges. We take this obligation seriously, and your complaint has prompted us to review the basis on which the monthly charge has been applied to your account.

We are also reviewing how your payments have been allocated and whether the statements you have received have contained all the information you are entitled to.

We will write to you again with our findings and our response to each of your concerns. We aim to do this as quickly as possible. In the meantime, if your circumstances change or you have any further concerns, please do not hesitate to contact us.

We are also aware that you are currently receiving Universal Credit and that this is a difficult time for you. If you would find it helpful to speak with a free, independent debt adviser, the MoneyHelper service can be reached at www.moneyhelper.org.uk or by calling 0800 138 7777.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I'm writing to complain about my home insurance renewal. In January, I received a promotional email from your bank's insurance division stating I could 'save up to 40% on my home insurance premium by switching to our new policy'. Based on this, I called your team and switched my policy. However, when I received my first premium statement, the cost was actually higher than my previous policy — not lower. When I called to query this on 3rd March, your agent apologised, said there had been a miscommunication in the marketing material, and offered to apply a one-off £25 goodwill credit to my account. I accepted this on the phone that same day as I was in a rush and just wanted it sorted. However, I've since had time to think about it and I'm not satisfied — the promotional email was clearly misleading and a £25 credit doesn't come close to covering the additional cost I'm now paying. I've also not received anything in writing confirming the resolution of my earlier call, or any information about what I can do if I'm unhappy. I believe the advertisement was dishonest and I want proper compensation for the increased premiums I'm now locked into paying.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues: (1) a potentially misleading financial promotion, and (2) complaints handling failures.

**1. Misleading financial promotion**
ICOBS 2.2.2R requires that when a firm communicates information, including a financial promotion, to a customer it must ensure it is clear, fair and not misleading. ICOBS 2.2.4G (guidance) specifically addresses financial promotions making pricing claims — including those that indicate or imply a firm can reduce a customer's costs — and states that such promotions should be consistent with the result reasonably expected to be achieved by the majority of customers who respond, and should state prominently the basis for any claimed benefits and any significant limitations. The customer's account that the email stated 'save up to 40%' but their premium actually increased is, on its face, a strong indicator that the promotion may have breached ICOBS 2.2.2R. The agent's own acknowledgement of a 'miscommunication in the marketing material' further supports this. You should retrieve and review the original promotional email to assess whether it met the ICOBS 2.2.2R standard and whether the ICOBS 2.2.4G criteria were satisfied.

**2. Complaints handling failures**
The customer states they received no written confirmation of the resolution of their 3rd March call and no information about their right to escalate if dissatisfied. The retrieved provisions do not include the DISP complaints handling rules (e.g. DISP 1.6R on final response letters and the requirement to include FOS referral rights), so I cannot cite specific DISP provisions here. I am flagging `insufficient_context` as true because the core complaints handling obligations (written final response, FOS referral rights, time limits) are not covered by the retrieved provisions. Human review is essential before sending.

**3. The £25 goodwill acceptance**
The customer accepted the £25 credit verbally on 3rd March but now wishes to reopen the matter. This raises a question about whether that acceptance constitutes a binding settlement. This is a legal/contractual question that goes beyond the retrieved FCA provisions and must be assessed by a senior handler or legal team before any commitment is made to the customer.

**4. Compensation**
The customer is seeking compensation for the difference in premiums. The retrieved provisions do not set out a redress methodology for misleading promotions in this context. Do not quantify or commit to any redress in the customer letter. This must be assessed by a senior handler.

**Recommended action:**
- Retrieve and review the original promotional email against ICOBS 2.2.2R and ICOBS 2.2.4G.
- Obtain legal/compliance input on whether the verbal acceptance of £25 on 3rd March is binding.
- Ensure a proper written response is issued that includes FOS referral rights (DISP requirements — not covered by retrieved provisions, so specialist input needed).
- Do not send the draft below without senior/legal review given the redress and settlement questions.

**Assumptions in the customer draft:** The draft below treats this as an open complaint (i.e. does not treat the 3rd March call as a final closed resolution), on the basis that no written final response was issued and the customer has formally re-raised dissatisfaction. If legal advice is that the verbal acceptance is binding, the draft must be revised significantly. The draft does not commit to any specific redress amount.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for taking the time to set out your concerns in detail. I am sorry to hear about your experience, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have raised two main concerns, which I will address in turn.

**The promotional email and your premium**

You received an email in January stating that you could save up to 40% on your home insurance premium by switching to our new policy. You switched on that basis, but your first premium statement showed a higher cost than your previous policy. Our agent acknowledged on 3rd March that there had been a miscommunication in the marketing material.

We are required to ensure that any information or financial promotion we send to customers — including pricing claims — is clear, fair and not misleading. Where a promotion refers to potential savings, it should reflect what customers can reasonably expect to achieve, and any significant limitations should be stated prominently. We take seriously your concern that the email you received may not have met this standard, and we are reviewing the original communication as part of our investigation into your complaint.

**The outcome of your 3rd March call**

You have told us that following your call on 3rd March, you accepted a £25 goodwill credit but did not receive anything in writing confirming the outcome, nor any information about what you could do if you remained dissatisfied. We apologise for this. You are entitled to receive a written response to your complaint and to be told about your options if you are not satisfied with our response.

**Next steps**

We are continuing to investigate your complaint. We will write to you again with our findings and our final response. At that point, if you remain dissatisfied, you will have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, and we will provide you with full details of how to do so.

We recognise that a £25 credit may not reflect the full extent of your concern, and we are not treating your complaint as closed. We will consider your complaint in full and respond to you in writing as quickly as possible.

Thank you again for raising this with us. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme frustration with how your bank has handled my situation. In January, I noticed that my current account had been debited with a £250 'account maintenance fee' that was never disclosed to me when I opened the account three years ago. The fee appeared without any prior notification or explanation. When I called your customer service team on 15 March to complain, the adviser acknowledged it was unusual and said someone would look into it and get back to me. I received a brief email the next day confirming receipt of my complaint, but it said nothing about timescales for a response or my right to go to the Ombudsman if I remained unhappy. It is now 20 May — over nine weeks since I complained — and I have heard absolutely nothing further. No update, no resolution, no explanation. I feel the bank has simply ignored me while continuing to benefit from the fee it took from my account. I am a pensioner on a fixed income and £250 is a significant sum for me. I believe the bank has acted unfairly by applying an undisclosed charge and then failing to deal with my complaint properly. I want the fee refunded and a proper explanation of why I have been treated this way.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that both require urgent attention:

**1. Procedural complaint-handling failures (high confidence)**
The acknowledgement email sent on 16 March failed to inform the customer of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. Crest Bank's own complaints-handling policy (retrieved chunk, customer-complaints-handling-procedure.md) states the bank will inform complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the FOS in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1. The complaint is now over nine weeks old with no substantive response, which on its face means the 8-week deadline for a final response has been missed (DISP 1.10B.13 references a 'final response' as the point at which a complaint is closed). A final response is now overdue.

**2. The undisclosed £250 account maintenance fee (moderate confidence — insufficient retrieved provisions)**
The retrieved provisions include CONC App 1.2.3R guidance on account fees and APR calculations for overdraft facilities, but do not directly address the obligation to disclose account maintenance fees at point of sale or the right to a refund of an undisclosed charge. The substantive merits of the fee complaint (whether it was properly disclosed, whether it should be refunded) cannot be fully assessed from the retrieved provisions alone. This must be reviewed by a senior handler or compliance before any commitment is made.

**3. Vulnerability indicator**
The customer has identified themselves as a pensioner on a fixed income for whom £250 is a significant sum. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be factored into how the complaint is handled and prioritised.

**Recommended immediate actions:**
- Issue a response today acknowledging the delay, confirming the complaint remains open, and informing the customer of their FOS referral rights (which should have been communicated earlier).
- Escalate the substantive fee question to compliance/a senior handler to assess whether the fee was properly disclosed and whether a refund is warranted — do not commit to a refund in the customer letter at this stage.
- Note the vulnerability indicator on the complaint file and apply appropriate care.
- The customer draft below is deliberately conservative on the fee merits: it acknowledges the complaint, apologises for the delay and the omission of FOS information, and commits to a substantive response — but does not promise a refund or admit the fee was wrongly applied, as those findings depend on a review of account opening documentation not available here.

**Assumption to confirm before sending:** The draft below treats the new letter as the vehicle for providing the FOS referral information that was omitted from the original acknowledgement. Please confirm this is appropriate under your internal DISP 1 procedures. Human review is required before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your complaint, and I am sorry for the way this matter has been handled so far.

I want to address two things straightaway.

First, our handling of your complaint. You raised your concerns with us on 15 March, and while we sent you an acknowledgement the following day, that acknowledgement did not tell you about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remained unhappy with our response. That was an omission on our part, and I apologise for it. I also recognise that it is now more than eight weeks since you complained and you have received no substantive update. That is not the standard of service we should be providing, and I am sorry.

I want to make sure you are aware of your rights now. If you are not satisfied with how we handle your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The Financial Ombudsman Service is an independent service that resolves disputes between customers and financial businesses. You can contact them at:

Financial Ombudsman Service
Exchange Tower
London E14 9SR
Telephone: 0800 023 4567
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk

Second, the substance of your complaint. You have told us that a £250 account maintenance fee was applied to your account without prior notification or explanation, and that this fee was not disclosed to you when you opened your account three years ago. We take this seriously. We are now reviewing the circumstances in which this fee was applied, including whether it was properly disclosed to you at the time your account was opened and subsequently.

We understand that £250 is a significant amount, and we will treat your complaint with the urgency it deserves. We will write to you again with our full findings and our response to your complaint as quickly as possible, and in any event within [insert date — please confirm the deadline under your internal DISP 1 procedures].

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us on [insert contact details].

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Crest Bank]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened a new savings account with your bank last month after seeing your promotional email comparing your interest rates to those of three competitor banks. The email claimed your rates were 'market-leading' and showed a comparison table, but it didn't mention that your rate was only a temporary introductory rate for 3 months, after which it drops to 0.5%. The competitors' rates shown were their standard ongoing rates. I feel this comparison was unfair and misleading — I wouldn't have switched if I'd known the rate would drop so dramatically. I transferred £40,000 from my previous provider and have now missed their loyalty bonus as a result. When I raised this issue at my branch three weeks ago, the branch manager told me it wasn't their responsibility because the email was sent by your 'digital marketing partner' and forwarded my complaint to them. I've heard nothing since from either party. I want to know who is actually dealing with my complaint and I want compensation for the interest I've lost by switching.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent.

**1. Potentially misleading financial promotion (BCOBS / CONC)**
The retrieved provisions support the following regulatory position:
- BCOBS 2 Annex 1R (via BCOBS 2.2A.1R and BCOBS 2.2A.2G) requires that where an introductory, promotional or preferential rate applies until a specified date or end of a fixed period, the summary box for a savings account must include details of this. The promotional email appears to have omitted this material information.
- BCOBS 4 (chunk bcobs4s1) provides guidance that where a firm notifies a banking customer of a material change to a rate of interest that will be to their disadvantage, the notice should refer to comparable products and the customer's ability to move. More directly, BCOBS 4 guidance states that where an introductory/promotional rate applies until a specified future date, the firm should provide notice of expiry within a reasonable period before it ceases to apply.
- The comparison table showing the bank's introductory rate against competitors' standard ongoing rates, without disclosing the temporary nature of the bank's rate, is likely to constitute a misleading financial promotion. CONC 3.3.11A G (chunk conc3s3) provides that a communication is likely to be misleading by omission if it refers to a zero percentage or low interest, introductory or other promotional offer in a way likely to influence a customer's decision, without including in a fair and prominent manner material information about relevant risks — including the limitations of the introductory offer, the date from which the lower rate would apply, and the rate thereafter. While CONC 3.3.11A technically relates to BNPL, the principle of misleading by omission in promotional comparisons is also reflected in BCOBS 2.2A guidance.

**2. Responsibility for the financial promotion**
The branch manager's suggestion that responsibility lies with the 'digital marketing partner' is unlikely to be a valid deflection. The bank, as the authorised firm, is responsible for financial promotions it communicates or approves, including those sent on its behalf by third parties. However, the retrieved provisions do not contain an explicit provision on this point, so this should be confirmed with your compliance/legal team before being stated to the customer.

**3. Complaint handling delay**
The customer raised the complaint at the branch three weeks ago and has heard nothing from either party. You should check your internal complaint records immediately to establish: (a) whether the complaint was formally logged; (b) whether any acknowledgement was sent; and (c) whether any regulatory deadlines (e.g. for a final response) are approaching or have been missed. The retrieved provisions do not include DISP complaint-handling timelines, so I cannot confirm the exact deadlines from this context — this is flagged as insufficient context on that specific point and must be checked against DISP 1 urgently.

**4. Compensation claim**
The customer is claiming compensation for lost interest (approximately the difference between the competitor's loyalty bonus and the bank's rate on £40,000). This is a financial redress question that must be assessed by a senior handler or complaints specialist. Do not commit to or quantify any redress in the customer letter at this stage.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft:**
- That the complaint has now been formally logged and the clock is running from the original branch contact date (three weeks ago).
- That the digital marketing partner was acting on the bank's behalf and the bank approved or communicated the promotional email — if so, the bank is the responsible firm.
- Whether any DISP deadlines have already been passed (this requires checking DISP 1, which is not in the retrieved context).
- The customer draft below does not admit liability, does not quantify redress, and does not include FOS referral prescribed wording (since it is not confirmed whether a final response is being issued or whether the 8-week deadline has been reached). If this letter is intended to serve as a final response, the FOS referral wording must be added — please confirm.

**Recommended action:** Log the complaint formally if not already done, escalate to your complaints team and compliance, and send the acknowledgement/interim response below. Do not send a final response without legal/compliance sign-off given the potential redress and misleading promotion issues.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for raising your concerns with us. I am writing to acknowledge your complaint and to let you know that we are taking it seriously.

I understand that you opened a savings account with us following a promotional email that compared our interest rate with those of three competitor banks. You have told us that the email did not make clear that our rate was a temporary introductory rate, and that the comparison was made against competitors' standard ongoing rates. You have also told us that, as a result of switching, you transferred £40,000 and have missed a loyalty bonus from your previous provider. I am sorry that your experience has fallen short of what you should expect.

I also want to address the question of who is responsible for handling your complaint. We take responsibility for communications sent on our behalf, and your complaint is being handled by us. I apologise that you were not given a clear answer to this when you visited the branch, and that you have not heard from us sooner.

We take our obligations around the accuracy and completeness of financial promotions seriously. Where a promotional or introductory interest rate applies for a limited period, customers are entitled to have that clearly communicated to them — including the rate that will apply afterwards and when the change will take effect. We are reviewing the promotional email you received against this standard.

We are now investigating your complaint fully. This includes reviewing the content of the promotional email, the comparison it contained, and the circumstances of your account switch. We will write to you again with our findings and our response to your request for compensation for the interest you say you have lost.

If you have any questions in the meantime, or if there is any further information you would like to provide, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Job Title]
[Bank Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Provides that where an introductory, promotional or preferential rate applies to a savings account until a specified date or end of a fixed period, the summary box (and by extension promotional materials) should include details of this, and that the rate or rates of interest must be included in the promotion itself rather than by reference to a separate document.

Requires that the summary box for a savings account include details of any introductory, promotional or preferential rate, including the circumstances in which it applies — relevant to whether the promotional email adequately disclosed the temporary nature of the rate.

Indicates that a communication referring to a zero percentage, low interest, introductory or other promotional offer is likely to be misleading by omission if it does not include in a fair and prominent manner material information about relevant risks, including the limitations of the offer, the date from which interest would change, and the rate thereafter — directly applicable to the promotional email's omission of the introductory rate's expiry.

claims.claim: BCOBS 4 guidance states that where an introductory/promotional rate applies until a specified future date, the firm should provide notice of expiry within a reasonable period before it ceases to apply.
claims.reasoning: Paragraph (5) of bcobs4s1 explicitly states that where an introductory, promotional or preferential rate of interest applies until a specified future date or end of a fixed period, a firm should provide notice of expiry within a reasonable period before that rate ceases to apply.

claims.claim: The comparison table showing the bank's introductory rate against competitors' standard ongoing rates, without disclosing the temporary nature of the bank's rate, is likely to constitute a misleading financial promotion.
claims.reasoning: CONC 3.3.11A G discusses misleading by omission when referring to introductory/promotional offers without including material information about relevant risks and limitations. However, this provision specifically relates to BNPL agreements, not savings accounts. The handler acknowledges this but argues the principle extends via BCOBS 2.2A guidance, which is a reasonable but not directly stated extension.

claims.claim: CONC 3.3.11A G provides that a communication is likely to be misleading by omission if it refers to a zero percentage or low interest, introductory or other promotional offer in a way likely to influence a customer's decision, without including in a fair and prominent manner material information about relevant risks — including the limitations of the introductory offer, the date from which the lower rate would apply, and the rate thereafter.
claims.reasoning: CONC 3.3.11A(2) and (4) in the retrieved provision directly state that a communication is likely to be misleading by omission if it refers to a zero percentage or low interest, introductory or other promotional offer likely to influence a customer's decision without including material information about relevant risks, including limitations of the offer, the date from which interest would accrue, and the rate.

claims.claim: CONC 3.3.11A technically relates to BNPL.
claims.reasoning: The provision explicitly states in (1) that it applies to communications and financial promotions in relation to BNPL agreements.

claims.claim: The bank, as the authorised firm, is responsible for financial promotions it communicates or approves, including those sent on its behalf by third parties.
claims.reasoning: While this is a well-known regulatory principle (from s.21 FSMA and related FCA rules), none of the retrieved provisions explicitly state this. The handler themselves acknowledges the retrieved provisions do not contain an explicit provision on this point.

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions do not include DISP complaint-handling timelines.
claims.reasoning: This is a factual statement about the absence of DISP provisions in the retrieved context, which is accurate — none of the retrieved provisions contain DISP complaint-handling timelines.

claims.claim: We take responsibility for communications sent on our behalf, and your complaint is being handled by us.
claims.reasoning: While this is a general regulatory principle regarding financial promotions responsibility, none of the retrieved provisions explicitly support the claim that the firm is responsible for communications sent on its behalf by third parties.

claims.claim: Where a promotional or introductory interest rate applies for a limited period, customers are entitled to have that clearly communicated to them — including the rate that will apply afterwards and when the change will take effect.
claims.reasoning: BCOBS 2 Annex 1R (Note 2 in bcobs2s6) requires that if an introductory, promotional, or preferential rate applies until a specified future date or end of a fixed period, the summary box should include details of this. Additionally, bcobs4s1 paragraph (5) requires notice of expiry. Together these support the claim that customers are entitled to clear communication about temporary rates, including what happens afterwards.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: My mother, who is 78 and has been diagnosed with vascular dementia, was granted a £5,000 credit card limit increase by your bank in March 2021. I am her son and hold lasting power of attorney for her financial affairs. At the time of the limit increase, she was already showing significant signs of confusion — she couldn't remember her own address when speaking to your staff on the phone, and her GP had written to you in February 2021 confirming her diagnosis. Despite this, your bank proceeded with the credit limit increase without taking any steps to help her understand the decision or contacting me as her attorney. She has since accumulated £4,800 in debt she cannot repay on a state pension. I raised this complaint with you in November 2024. Your branch manager told me verbally that because the credit limit increase happened over three years ago, you won't look into it and said it's 'time-barred'. I believe you should have recognised her lack of mental capacity and never increased her limit. I want the debt written off and a proper explanation of why you are refusing to investigate.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This is a serious complaint involving potential regulatory breaches under CONC 6.7.7R and CONC 2.10.10G, as well as possible failure to act on clear indicators of mental capacity limitation under CONC 2.10.8G (indicators listed in conc2s10 chunk). The key facts are stark: the bank received a GP letter in February 2021 confirming a dementia diagnosis, the customer could not recall her own address during the phone call, and the limit increase proceeded anyway. These are precisely the kinds of indicators CONC 2.10 identifies as requiring the firm to pause and assess capacity before proceeding.

On the 'time-bar' point: the branch manager's verbal assertion that the complaint is time-barred is not supported by anything in the retrieved provisions. You must not rely on that position without proper legal and compliance review. The retrieved provisions do not contain DISP time-limit rules, so I cannot confirm or deny the precise complaint-handling deadline position from this context alone — this must be checked against DISP rules by your compliance team before any time-bar position is communicated to the customer.

On the substance: CONC 6.7.7R prohibits a firm from increasing a credit limit where a customer is at risk of financial difficulties, and CONC 2.10.10G indicates firms should have suitable practices for fair treatment of customers with mental capacity limitations. The indicators present here (GP diagnosis letter, inability to recall address) are squarely within the examples in CONC 2.10.8G. The failure to contact the LPA holder before proceeding is also a serious concern.

On the requested remedy (debt write-off): this is a financial redress decision that I cannot make or recommend. It must be assessed by a senior complaints handler or your financial redress team.

Human review is required before anything is sent. Key assumptions to confirm: (1) whether the GP letter of February 2021 is on file; (2) whether the call recording from March 2021 exists and shows the address confusion; (3) whether the LPA was registered with the bank at the time; (4) the correct DISP time-limit position — do not rely on the branch manager's verbal time-bar assertion without compliance sign-off; (5) whether a formal final response has previously been issued. The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint, commits to investigation, and does not pre-judge the time-bar or the remedy — it is deliberately conservative pending those checks.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer's name / 'Mr [Surname]'],

Thank you for writing to us about the concerns you have raised on behalf of your mother. We are sorry to hear about her situation and recognise how distressing this must be for your family.

We have received your complaint, which we understand relates to the credit limit increase applied to your mother's credit card account in March 2021, and to the way in which that decision was made given her health circumstances at the time. You have also raised concerns about the response you received from our branch manager regarding whether this complaint would be investigated.

We want to be clear: we take complaints of this nature very seriously. Our regulatory obligations require us to treat customers fairly, and this includes customers who may have, or who we have reason to believe may have, a mental capacity limitation. We are also required not to increase a customer's credit limit where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties. Where indicators of a mental capacity limitation are present — such as a medical diagnosis communicated to us, or signs of confusion during a customer interaction — we are expected to have appropriate practices in place to respond to those indicators before taking decisions that affect the customer's account.

We are now investigating your complaint fully. As part of that investigation, we will review the information available to us at the time of the credit limit increase, including any correspondence received from your mother's GP and records of her interactions with us. We will also consider the concerns you have raised about not being contacted as her attorney.

We will write to you again with our findings. If we are unable to provide a final response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint, we will write to explain why and to let you know when we expect to be able to respond.

Regarding the branch manager's comment about the complaint being 'time-barred': we are not in a position to confirm that position at this stage. Our investigation will consider all relevant matters, and we will explain our position fully in our final response.

If, once you have received our final response, you remain dissatisfied, you will have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about that right when we issue our final response.

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Prohibits a firm from increasing a customer's credit limit on a credit card where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties — directly applicable given the customer's circumstances at the time of the March 2021 increase.

Indicates that firms should take reasonable steps to ensure they have suitable business practices for the fair treatment of customers who they understand, or reasonably suspect, have or may have a mental capacity limitation — relevant to the failure to act on the GP letter and signs of confusion before proceeding with the limit increase.

Indicates that a firm would not act inappropriately by not significantly increasing the amount of credit where it reasonably believes the decision would be voidable at the instance of the customer — supports the concern that the limit increase should not have proceeded given the known diagnosis.

Requires a firm to suspend pursuit of debt recovery where it has been notified that the customer might not have mental capacity — relevant to the ongoing debt position and the bank's obligations now that the capacity limitation is formally raised.

Reminds firms of the requirement under CONC 7.2.1R to have policies and procedures for fair and appropriate treatment of vulnerable customers — relevant to the systemic failure alleged in this complaint.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened an execution-only share dealing account with your firm last year to buy and sell shares listed on the London Stock Exchange. When I signed up, I was never told anything about your complaints procedure — no leaflet, no mention of it at all. I wasn't even told about the Financial Ombudsman Service. More recently, I purchased units in a structured product through the same account. The product turned out to be far more complex than I expected — it embeds a derivative and I don't understand the risks at all. Nobody at your firm asked me any questions about my knowledge or experience before letting me buy it, and I wasn't warned that you weren't assessing whether it was appropriate for me. I've now lost a significant amount of money. I believe your firm failed in its obligations both by not assessing appropriateness for a complex product and by never informing me of my rights to complain or access the ombudsman.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues. I can partially address them from the retrieved provisions, but there are significant gaps that require human review before any response is sent.

**Issue 1: Failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale**
DISP 1.2.1R (chunk: disp1s2) requires respondents to provide eligible complainants with information about their internal complaints handling procedures, including a reference to the Financial Ombudsman Service, at or immediately after the point of first contact. DISP 1.2.3G (chunk: disp1s2) indicates that summary details should cover how the firm handles complaints and that unresolved complaints may be referred to the FOS. If the customer was never given this information at account opening, there is a prima facie breach of DISP 1.2.1R. This is a binding rule obligation.

**Issue 2: Appropriateness assessment for the structured product / complex instrument**
The customer alleges no appropriateness assessment was conducted before they purchased a structured product embedding a derivative, and that they were not warned that appropriateness was not being assessed. The retrieved provisions do not include the relevant COBS rules on appropriateness (e.g. COBS 10 or COBS 10A). The chunk from COBS 4 (cobs4s16) touches on FSCS/FOS disclosures for complex investments but does not set out the appropriateness assessment obligations. The MCOB execution-only provisions (mcob4s18) relate to regulated mortgage contracts, not share dealing or structured products. The MiFID complaints section (disp1s20) is relevant context but does not supply the appropriateness rules themselves. I therefore cannot ground a regulatory position on the appropriateness allegation from the retrieved provisions alone — `insufficient_context` is set to true for that limb.

**Recommended action:**
1. Confirm whether the customer was provided with any complaints procedure information at account opening (check onboarding records).
2. Obtain the relevant COBS 10 / COBS 10A appropriateness rules before assessing the second limb — do not make any admission or denial on appropriateness in the customer letter at this stage.
3. The complaint involves a significant financial loss, potential regulatory breaches, and possible redress — mandatory human review before sending.
4. Note: the customer's message as written reads as if it is the customer's own complaint text rather than a handler's summary. I have treated it as the substance of the complaint for drafting purposes. Please confirm this is correct.
5. The draft below acknowledges the complaint and confirms it is under investigation. It does not admit liability, quantify redress, or make commitments on appropriateness that the retrieved provisions do not support. The FOS referral right and time limit wording is included only in general terms consistent with DISP 1.2.3G — the specific time limits (e.g. 6 months from final response) are not in the retrieved provisions and have not been stated as a precise figure.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
- That this is a final response letter (if so, prescribed FOS referral wording under DISP 1.6R would normally be required — that rule is not in the retrieved provisions and the draft does not include prescribed wording).
- Whether the 8-week deadline for a final response has been reached.
- Whether the customer is an 'eligible complainant' under DISP (almost certainly yes for a retail customer, but confirm).
- Whether the structured product was a MiFID instrument (relevant to which appropriateness regime applies).
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take the concerns you have raised very seriously.

We have registered your complaint, which covers two matters: first, that you were not provided with information about our complaints procedure or your right to refer complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service when you opened your account; and second, that we did not assess whether the structured product you purchased was appropriate for you, and did not warn you that no such assessment was being carried out.

We are now investigating both aspects of your complaint carefully and will write to you again with our findings.

On the first point, we are required to make information about our internal complaints handling procedure available to you, including information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, at or around the time we first deal with you. We are reviewing our records to establish what information was provided to you at account opening.

On the second point, we are reviewing the circumstances of your purchase of the structured product, including what steps were taken before the transaction was completed.

We will provide you with a full response as soon as our investigation is complete. In the meantime, if you have any questions or wish to provide us with any further information, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

If you are not satisfied with our final response, you may have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about this when we write to you with our final response.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Firm name]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I have a regulated mortgage with your bank and I'm writing to complain about two issues. First, when I fell into arrears last year, you applied a £250 'arrears management fee' each month for three consecutive months, plus compound interest on those fees. I've checked with other lenders and nobody charges anything close to that — most charge £25-50 at most. I believe these charges are grossly excessive and the compound interest on a second charge mortgage is unlawful. Second, when I tried to call your complaints line to raise this issue, the only number provided was an 0871 number. I was on hold for 45 minutes and the call cost me over £18. I shouldn't have to pay a premium rate number just to make a complaint. I'm currently on long-term sick leave following a cancer diagnosis and am struggling financially as a result of reduced income. I'd like a full refund of the excessive charges, the interest applied to them, and reimbursement of my phone costs.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises three distinct regulatory issues, all of which have clear grounding in the retrieved provisions. There is also a significant vulnerability indicator (cancer diagnosis, financial hardship) that requires careful handling throughout.

**Issue 1: Arrears management charges (£250/month)**
MCOB 12.4.1 (not directly retrieved but MCOB 12.4 is referenced in the retrieved provisions) governs payment shortfall charges. The retrieved provisions in MCOB 12.4.4R set out costs that must NOT be included when calculating payment shortfall charges — including funding/capital costs, general bank charges not incurred as a result of the payment shortfall, unrecovered fees, advertising costs, and regulatory fines. MCOB 12.5.1R and 12.5.2R require that charges must not be excessive. MCOB 12.5.3G sets out factors for assessing excessiveness: comparison with market charges, degree of abuse of trust, and disclosure. The customer's comparison evidence (£25–50 at comparable lenders vs £250) is directly relevant to MCOB 12.5.3G(1). This is a strong complaint on the merits and warrants investigation. You will need to review your cost-of-administration calculation to confirm whether the £250 fee is genuinely cost-reflective under MCOB 12.4.4R.

**Issue 2: Compound interest on arrears charges (second charge mortgage)**
MCOB 12.5.5R is directly on point: a second charge lender may ONLY charge simple interest on charges applied for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract. If this is a second charge mortgage and compound interest was applied to the arrears management fees, this is a breach of a binding rule. You must confirm whether this is a first or second charge mortgage — the customer says 'regulated mortgage' but does not specify. If it is a second charge, the compound interest element is unlawful under MCOB 12.5.5R and must be refunded. If it is a first charge, MCOB 12.5.5R does not apply, but the excessive charges rules still do.

**Issue 3: 0871 complaints line**
GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm must not bind a consumer to pay more than the basic rate for calls to contact the firm in relation to a contract. GEN 7.2.3R defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection, providing no contribution to the firm's costs or revenues. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant. This is a clear breach of GEN 7.2.1R. The customer is entitled to reimbursement of the excess call cost (i.e., the amount above basic rate). You should also rectify the complaints line number going forward.

**Vulnerability**
The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship from reduced income. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The response must reflect appropriate sensitivity and the firm's obligations to treat vulnerable customers fairly (Principle 6, referenced in MCOB 12.2.1G). Ensure your vulnerability policy is applied and that any investigation is expedited.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. Whether this is a first or second charge regulated mortgage — this determines whether MCOB 12.5.5R applies to the compound interest issue.
2. Whether the £250 fee can be justified by a cost-of-administration calculation under MCOB 12.4.4R.
3. The actual excess call cost above basic rate for the 0871 call.
4. Whether a financial redress decision is being made — if so, this must go through your redress/compensation process and requires senior sign-off.

Human review is required before sending given: potential financial redress across multiple heads of claim, confirmed customer vulnerability, and a possible rule breach (MCOB 12.5.5R on compound interest). The customer draft below is written on the assumption that the bank is acknowledging the complaints and committing to investigate — it does NOT admit liability or quantify redress, as those decisions must be made after investigation.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing, and we want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have raised three concerns, which we address in turn.

**Arrears management charges**

You have told us that you were charged £250 per month for three months in arrears management fees, and that comparable lenders charge significantly less. Our rules require that charges applied to customers in payment difficulties must not be excessive and must reflect the actual additional administration costs we incur — they cannot include items such as general overhead costs, funding costs, or regulatory fines. We are required to investigate whether the charges applied to your account meet that standard, including by reference to what similar lenders charge for comparable services. We will review your account and write to you with our findings.

**Interest applied to arrears charges**

You have also raised a concern about the interest applied to those charges. We are reviewing whether the basis on which interest was calculated was appropriate for your type of mortgage. We will address this as part of our investigation and confirm our position to you in our final response.

**Cost of calling our complaints line**

We are sorry that you were directed to an 0871 number to raise your complaint. Our rules require that any telephone line we operate for customers to contact us about their contract must not cost more than the basic rate of connection — and 0871 numbers do not meet that standard. We should not have directed you to that number for this purpose. We will look into the cost you incurred and address reimbursement of the excess call charge in our final response.

**Next steps**

We are now investigating all three aspects of your complaint. We will aim to provide you with a full written response as quickly as possible. If you have not heard from us and wish to follow up, please contact us using [basic-rate or freephone number].

If you are not satisfied with our final response, or if you have not received one within eight weeks of the date you first raised your complaint, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further information about this right when we send our final response.

Thank you again for bringing these matters to our attention.

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Job title]
[Date]
