fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with your bank's handling of my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £487 applied to my current account in March 2024. I originally submitted my complaint on 15 April 2024. I received your final response letter dated 28 May 2024 which rejected my complaint, stating the charges were correctly applied under the account terms and conditions. However, I believe your response was inadequate and unfair. You failed to properly consider that I was in hospital at the time the charges accrued and had no ability to manage my account. Furthermore, I note that your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It simply said I could 'contact the ombudsman if unhappy' without any further detail about the time limits for doing so. I want to know what my rights are and whether I can still escalate this matter, as it has now been approximately five months since I received your response.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a handler. I am treating it as a complaint escalation for the handler to review and act upon.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Deficient final response:** The customer's allegation that the final response letter dated 28 May 2024 did not include a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, did not provide the FOS website address, and did not properly inform them of the time limits for referral appears to constitute a breach of DISP 1.6.2R. That rule requires a final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS. This must be confirmed by reviewing the actual letter sent.

2. **FOS referral time limit:** Under DISP 2.8.2R, the complainant ordinarily has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. The final response was dated 28 May 2024 and approximately five months have elapsed. The six-month window is therefore still open but closing imminently — the handler must treat this as urgent. However, if the final response was deficient (i.e. it did not include the required FOS information), there is a question as to whether the six-month clock has properly started running at all. This is a legal/regulatory question that requires human review before any position is communicated to the customer.

3. **Unarranged overdraft charges:** The charges of £487 relate to unarranged overdraft charges. CONC 5C.3.1R and CONC 5C.3.2R are relevant: if the charges exceeded what would have been payable under an equivalent arranged overdraft, they may be unenforceable and recoverable. The customer's original complaint should be reviewed against these provisions. Additionally, BCOBS 8.4.13R requires the firm to have sent an alert when the account entered unarranged overdraft. Whether such alerts were sent should be checked.

4. **Vulnerability:** The customer states they were hospitalised at the time the charges accrued. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be factored into the review of the original complaint and any redress assessment. This alone requires human review.

5. **Redress threshold:** The charges total £487, which is below the £500 threshold in Crest Bank's internal policy requiring Head of Customer Operations sign-off, but the vulnerability indicator and potential regulatory significance of the deficient final response letter may bring it within the 'regulatory significance' limb of that policy regardless.

**Recommended actions:**
1. Urgently retrieve and review the original final response letter of 28 May 2024 to confirm whether it was deficient under DISP 1.6.2R.
2. Treat the FOS referral window as closing imminently (around late November 2024 on a six-month count from 28 May 2024) — advise the customer of their rights without delay.
3. Review whether the unarranged overdraft charges complied with CONC 5C.3.1R and whether BCOBS 8.4.13R alerts were sent.
4. Apply vulnerability considerations to the complaint review given the customer's hospitalisation.
5. This response must be reviewed by a senior handler or the Head of Customer Operations before sending, given the vulnerability indicator, the potential regulatory breach in the original response, and the financial redress dimension.

**Assumption to confirm:** The draft below assumes the final response was indeed deficient as alleged. If the letter did in fact include the required FOS information, the customer-facing text will need to be revised accordingly.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with our response to your complaint about unarranged overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024, and we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time.

We take our obligations to customers very seriously, and we want to make sure you are fully informed of your rights.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our final response dated 28 May 2024, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You should be aware that there is a time limit for doing so — ordinarily six months from the date of our final response — so we would encourage you to act promptly if you wish to pursue this route.

The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted as follows:
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

We are also enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, which sets out how the service works and the time limits that apply.

**Review of your complaint**

We are also reviewing the concerns you have raised, including the circumstances in which the charges were applied and the personal difficulties you experienced at the time. We will write to you separately with the outcome of that review.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

Sets the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which the complainant must refer their complaint to the FOS — relevant because approximately five months have elapsed since the final response dated 28 May 2024.

Prohibits firms from charging a customer using an unarranged overdraft more than they would have paid under an equivalent arranged overdraft — relevant to whether the £487 charges were lawfully applied.

Provides that any unarranged overdraft charge imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R is unenforceable and recoverable by the customer — relevant to the potential remedy if the charges were unlawfully applied.

Requires the firm to send an alert to the customer when their personal current account has entered or will enter unarranged overdraft — relevant to whether the bank met its obligations before the charges accrued.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in March 2024. At no point during the application process or at the point of sale was I given any information about your complaints procedure or told how I could complain if something went wrong. I've since had a problem with an overcharge on my account and when I tried to find out how to complain, there was nothing on my loan agreement, nothing in the paperwork I was given, and I had to spend over an hour on the phone just to find out where to send my complaint. I also notice your website has no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service anywhere. I believe you have failed in your duty to make me aware of my rights and I want this addressed.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues under DISP 1.2.1R (Consumer awareness rules):

1. **Failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale**: DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b) requires respondents to refer eligible complainants to the availability of complaints procedure information in writing at, or immediately after, the point of sale (for non-payment service products). A personal loan taken out in March 2024 would fall under this obligation. The customer states they received nothing in their loan agreement or accompanying paperwork — if accurate, this is a breach of DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b).

2. **Failure to provide FOS information on the website**: DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a) requires respondents to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including its website address) on the respondent's website, where one exists. The customer states there is no mention of the FOS on your website — if accurate, this is a breach of DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a).

3. **Failure to provide complaints procedure information on request/when acknowledging the complaint**: DISP 1.2.1R(3) requires that complaints procedure information be provided in writing and free of charge on request and when acknowledging a complaint. The customer's account of spending over an hour on the phone to find out how to complain is relevant here.

**Recommended action**: You should investigate whether the point-of-sale documentation for this customer's March 2024 loan included the required reference to complaints procedure information, and whether your website currently carries FOS information. If either failure is confirmed, you should acknowledge the breach, apologise, and confirm remedial steps. You should also check whether the underlying overcharge complaint has been or is being handled separately.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft**:
- Was the personal loan a payment service? If so, DISP 1.2.1R(2)(a) (rather than (b)) would apply, referencing Payment Services Regulations requirements. The draft below assumes it is not a payment service.
- Has the overcharge complaint been formally logged and acknowledged separately? The customer mentions this as the underlying issue — it may need its own response track.
- Does your website in fact carry FOS information? If it does, that element of the complaint may not be upheld.
- This complaint involves a potential regulatory breach finding and may have redress implications (e.g. time and inconvenience). Human review is required before sending.

Note: DISP 1.2.1R also requires complaints procedure information to be published (DISP 1.2.1R(1)) and provided free of charge on request (DISP 1.2.1R(3)). The customer's difficulty in obtaining this information is relevant to both obligations.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for raising these concerns. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced when trying to find out how to make a complaint, and I want to assure you that we take this seriously.

You have raised two specific issues, and I want to address each in turn.

**Information about our complaints process at the point of sale**

We are required to make information about our internal complaints procedure available to customers, and to refer customers to that information in writing at or around the time they take out a product with us. We are also required to provide that information in writing, free of charge, when a customer asks for it or when we acknowledge a complaint. I understand that you did not receive this information with your loan documentation in March 2024, and that you had significant difficulty obtaining it when you needed it. I apologise for this. We are investigating what happened and will confirm our findings to you.

**Information about the Financial Ombudsman Service on our website**

We are also required to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service — an independent, free service that can consider unresolved complaints — on our website. You have told us that you could not find this information. We are looking into this as a matter of urgency.

**Your underlying complaint about an overcharge**

I note that the reason you needed to find our complaints process was because of a problem with an overcharge on your account. Please be assured that this has been logged as a separate complaint and you will receive a response to that matter in due course. [Please amend or remove this sentence if the overcharge complaint has not yet been formally logged.]

**Next steps**

We will write to you with the outcome of our investigation into the concerns raised in this letter. In the meantime, if you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us.

If you are not satisfied with how we handle your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. Further information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, including how to contact them, is available at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]

structure-titan.scenario_text: I've been trying to call my bank to make a formal complaint about an incorrect fee they charged me. The only number they provide for complaints is an 0871 number, which is costing me a fortune every time I ring. I've called three times now and been on hold for extended periods, racking up charges. I'm on a basic state pension and these call costs are adding up significantly. I shouldn't have to pay premium rates just to complain. I want the bank to reimburse me for the call charges I've incurred and to provide a proper free or local-rate number for complaints.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues, both well-supported by the retrieved provisions.

**1. Use of an 0871 number for complaints — clear regulatory breach**

DISP 1.3.1AAR requires that where a respondent operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling an eligible complainant to submit a complaint, the complainant must not be bound to pay more than the basic rate. DISP 1.3.1ADR explicitly lists numbers beginning with the prefix 0871 as non-compliant. The bank's use of an 0871 number for complaints is therefore a breach of DISP 1.3.1AAR on its face.

Additionally, GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.5R also lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant. This provides a second, overlapping basis for the breach.

**2. Reimbursement of call charges incurred**

The retrieved provisions establish the obligation not to charge above basic rate, but do not themselves prescribe a specific remedy or quantum of reimbursement for charges already incurred. You should flag this to your complaints manager and legal/compliance team before committing to a specific redress figure. The customer's request for reimbursement is reasonable in principle given the breach, but the amount and mechanism for redress is a decision for a human reviewer.

**3. Vulnerability indicator**

The customer has indicated they are on a basic state pension and that the accumulated call charges are significant to them. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and should be noted on the complaint record. The response and any redress decision should be handled with appropriate care.

**Recommended action:**
- Acknowledge the complaint and the regulatory position on 0871 numbers.
- Confirm the bank will provide a compliant number (0800, 01/02/03, or similar) for future contact.
- Do NOT commit in the customer letter to a specific reimbursement amount — flag this for human review and redress assessment.
- Route to human review before sending, given the potential redress element and vulnerability indicator.

**Assumptions to confirm:**
- That this is indeed Crest Bank's own complaints line (not a third-party number).
- The actual call charges incurred (the customer should be asked to provide itemised evidence if not already held).
- Whether the customer has already submitted a formal complaint through another channel, or whether this message itself constitutes the complaint.
- Whether the 'next business day' informal resolution route under DISP 1.5 is available — given three calls and extended hold times, it almost certainly is not, and a full complaint process should be followed.

Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced trying to reach us, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously.

Your complaint has been logged and you have been assigned the reference number [REFERENCE NUMBER]. Your complaint is being handled by [Handler Name], who can be contacted at [contact details].

We are required by our regulator to ensure that customers are not charged more than the basic rate when calling us to make a complaint. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this requirement, and we recognise that the number you have been using should not have been provided for this purpose. We apologise unreservedly for this.

We are looking into the call charges you have incurred as a result, and we will write to you again once we have completed our investigation. In the meantime, if you need to contact us further about this complaint, please use [COMPLIANT NUMBER — e.g. 0800 / 01 / 02 / 03 number to be inserted by handler].

We aim to provide you with our final response as soon as possible. If you are not satisfied with our response once you receive it, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about this right when we send our final response.

Thank you for your patience.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in 2021 and was sold payment protection insurance at the same time. In March this year, I submitted a formal complaint explaining that the PPI was mis-sold because I already had employer sickness cover and was never asked about existing protection. Your complaints team sent me a final response letter four weeks later rejecting my complaint, but the letter simply stated 'we have reviewed your complaint and do not believe it should be upheld' with no further explanation. There was no assessment of the specific points I raised about my existing employer cover, no consideration of whether redress might be appropriate, and no explanation of how you reached your decision. I feel my complaint has not been properly investigated or fairly assessed, and the response I received was neither clear nor informative. I would like my complaint to be reconsidered properly.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is complaining that their PPI mis-selling complaint (relating to a 2021 loan sale) was rejected without adequate explanation or proper assessment of the specific points raised — namely that they already had employer sickness cover and were never asked about existing protection.

Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:

1. DISP App 3.1.1G confirms that DISP Appendix 3 applies to complaints about the sale of a payment protection contract, including where the sale was non-advised, and regardless of when the sale took place. The 2021 sale falls within scope.

2. DISP App 3.2.6G indicates the firm should take into account all information it holds about the sale and consider other relevant issues. A response that ignores the customer's specific point about existing employer cover would not meet this expectation.

3. DISP App 3.5.1E requires the firm, as part of its investigation, to determine whether any claim on the policy was rejected and whether the complainant may have reasonably expected it to be paid. While no claim rejection is mentioned here, the evidential provision underscores the need for a thorough investigation.

4. DISP App 3.6.2E sets out circumstances in which the firm should presume the customer would not have bought the PPI — including where the firm did not take reasonable steps to ensure the customer only bought a policy for which they were eligible to claim benefits (sub-paragraph 6), and where the firm did not take reasonable care to ensure the policy was suitable for the customer's demands and needs (sub-paragraph 5 for advised sales). The customer's point about existing employer cover is directly relevant to eligibility and suitability.

Recommended action: The complaint should be reopened and properly reinvestigated. The final response letter as described does not appear to have addressed the substance of the complaint — specifically the customer's existing employer sickness cover and whether they were asked about it. The firm should assess whether the sale was suitable and whether the customer was eligible to claim, applying the DISP App 3 framework. A new, reasoned final response should be issued.

Assumptions/uncertainties to confirm before sending the customer draft:
- Confirm that the original final response letter genuinely contained no substantive reasoning (as the customer describes). If it did contain reasoning not mentioned by the customer, the approach may differ.
- Confirm whether the sale was advised or non-advised, as this affects which limbs of DISP App 3.6.2E apply most directly.
- Confirm whether the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service after the rejection — if so, the FOS may already have jurisdiction and the firm should take legal/compliance advice before reopening.
- This complaint involves potential redress and a financial decision, so human review is required before sending.

Note: The PPI deadline provisions (DISP 2.8.9R, DISP App 3.11) relate to sales on or before 29 August 2017 and the 29 August 2019 referral deadline. As this sale was in 2021, those time-bar provisions do not apply. The standard DISP complaints handling framework applies instead. Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry that you feel your complaint was not properly investigated and that our response did not address the specific concerns you raised.

Having reviewed your complaint, we agree that your concerns deserve a fuller and more considered response. You told us that at the time of your loan in 2021 you already had employer sickness cover in place, and that you were not asked about any existing protection before the payment protection insurance was sold to you. These are important points that go to the heart of whether the policy was suitable for your needs and whether you were eligible to benefit from it — and they should have been addressed in our earlier response.

We are reopening your complaint so that it can be properly reinvestigated. We will consider all of the information you have provided, including your point about your existing employer cover, and we will write to you again with a full response that explains our findings and how we reached them.

We will aim to complete our review as quickly as possible and will keep you informed of our progress. If you have any further information you would like us to take into account — for example, details of your employer sickness cover at the time of the sale — please do send this to us.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Contact details]

structure-titan.scenario_text: I called my bank on Monday to complain about an incorrect £15 fee on my current account. The adviser I spoke to agreed it was an error, reversed the charge immediately, and I told her I was happy with that resolution. However, it's now been over two weeks and I haven't received anything in writing from the bank confirming that my complaint was resolved or telling me about my rights to go to the Ombudsman if I change my mind. I feel the bank hasn't followed proper procedure — I'd like confirmation of what should have been sent to me and why it wasn't.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is describing a complaint that appears to have been resolved by close of business on the next business day (the 'next business day rule'). However, the complaint was received on a Monday by phone, and the fee was reversed immediately during that same call — so it is likely this was resolved same-day or next business day, which would engage DISP 1.5 as referenced in your internal procedure.

Key regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:

1. **Logging**: Under your internal procedure (referencing DISP 1.5), a complaint resolved by close of business on the next business day may be handled under the 'next business day rule', but it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted. You should check whether this complaint was logged.

2. **Written confirmation / summary resolution communication**: The customer states they received nothing in writing. Your internal procedure requires a written acknowledgement within five business days of receipt (referencing DISP 1.6.2R). More importantly, DISP 2.8.3G (Guidance) makes clear that the six-month FOS referral time limit is only triggered by a response which is a 'final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication', and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit. If no such communication was sent, the six-month FOS referral clock has not started running for the customer.

3. **FOS referral rights**: Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in your internal procedure sections 7.4 and 7.5), the customer must be informed of their right to refer to the FOS, including the six-month time limit from the date of the final response, and the FOS contact details.

**Recommended action**: You should issue a written response to the customer now — this can serve as the summary resolution communication or final response — confirming: (a) the complaint was received and resolved; (b) the £15 fee was reversed; (c) the customer's right to refer to the FOS if they remain dissatisfied, including the six-month time limit from the date of this letter and FOS contact details.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft**:
- Confirm the complaint was resolved on the same day or by close of business the next business day (i.e., Monday or Tuesday). If it was not resolved within that window, different acknowledgement obligations may apply.
- Confirm the complaint has been logged in the CMS.
- The financial redress here is £15, which is below the £500 threshold requiring Head of Customer Operations countersignature under your internal procedure — but please verify this is the only redress involved.
- The customer draft below treats this as a final response / summary resolution communication. If your compliance team considers a different classification appropriate, the letter should be adjusted accordingly.

Human review is recommended before sending, as the draft depends on the assumption that the complaint was resolved within the next business day window and that no further redress is in scope.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch. I am writing to confirm that we received your complaint on [date of call] regarding an incorrect £15 fee applied to your current account.

I understand that when you spoke with our adviser on that day, she agreed the charge had been applied in error and reversed it immediately. I am pleased that this was resolved to your satisfaction at the time.

I am sorry that you did not receive written confirmation of this sooner. I want to make sure you have a clear record of what happened and that you are aware of your rights.

**Our findings**
Having reviewed the matter, we uphold your complaint. The £15 fee was applied incorrectly and has been reversed in full. We consider this to be the appropriate resolution.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**
If you are dissatisfied with our response, or if you change your mind about the resolution, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at:
- Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- Telephone: 0800 023 4567

If you have any questions about this letter or your complaint, please do not hesitate to contact us using the reference number above.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank
[Date]

Establishes that the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the respondent sent its final response, making it essential that the final response is issued and correctly dated.

Indicates that the six-month FOS referral time limit is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response or summary resolution communication, and that response must tell the complainant about the time limit — relevant here because no such communication was previously sent.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I made a formal complaint to my bank on 14 March about an incorrect fee applied to my current account. I received a brief email the same day saying 'thanks, we've got your complaint' but it didn't tell me anything about when I could expect a response or what the process would be. It's now been three weeks and I've heard absolutely nothing since that initial email — no updates, no indication of progress, nothing. I feel like my complaint has just been forgotten about. I want to know what's happening and when I'll get a proper answer.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer submitted a formal complaint on 14 March. Based on the retrieved provisions, there are two immediate issues to address:

1. **Acknowledgement deficiency**: Under Crest Bank's internal procedure (referencing DISP 1.6.2R), a written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt, confirming the complaint reference number, the handler's name and contact details, and the expected resolution timescale. The initial 'thanks, we've got your complaint' email does not appear to have met these requirements — it lacked the reference number, handler details, and resolution timescale. This should be remedied promptly.

2. **Eight-week deadline**: Under DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must send either a final response or a written holding response (explaining why a final response cannot yet be issued and informing the customer of their FOS referral right) by the end of eight weeks from 14 March — i.e., by approximately 9 May. Three weeks have elapsed; there is still time to respond properly, but the customer should receive an update now.

Recommended action: Send the customer a proper acknowledgement/update letter that (a) confirms the complaint reference number, (b) names the handler, (c) explains the investigation is under way, and (d) gives the eight-week deadline by which a final response will be issued. If the complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, escalate to the Head of Customer Operations per the internal procedure.

Assumptions to confirm before sending the draft below:
- Has a complaint reference number been assigned in the CMS? If not, one must be created and logged today.
- Has the complaint been formally logged in the CMS on the date of receipt (14 March)? If not, this needs to be rectified.
- Is the complaint straightforward enough to be handled by the assigned handler, or does it require escalation?
- The draft below does not include FOS referral rights in prescribed form, as this is an interim update rather than a final response or eight-week holding response — those rights are required at the final response stage or at eight weeks under DISP 1.6.2R.

Human review is recommended before sending, as the adequacy of the original acknowledgement and the CMS logging status need to be confirmed.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch on 14 March about the fee applied to your current account. I am sorry that our initial reply did not give you the information you needed about what would happen next — I understand how frustrating it must be to have heard nothing further, and I want to put that right now.

Your complaint has been formally recorded and is being investigated. Your complaint reference number is [REFERENCE NUMBER]. The member of our team handling your complaint is [HANDLER NAME], who can be contacted at [CONTACT DETAILS].

We are looking into the fee you have raised and will write to you with our findings. We are required to send you a full written response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. On that basis, you can expect to hear from us with our final response no later than [DATE — eight weeks from 14 March].

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact [HANDLER NAME] using the details above.

Yours sincerely,

[HANDLER NAME]
[JOB TITLE]
Crest Bank

structure-titan.scenario_text: I made a complaint to my bank, Westfield Banking PLC, six weeks ago about a payment protection insurance policy that was sold to me through their branch but was actually underwritten by a separate insurer. Three weeks after I complained, the bank wrote to me saying they were forwarding my complaint to the insurer because the insurer was solely responsible. However, the bank didn't tell me the insurer's contact details in that letter — just said 'the relevant insurer' without a name, address, or phone number. I also feel that three weeks was not 'prompt' — I was left waiting with no update for all that time. I've now heard nothing from the insurer either, and I don't even know who to chase. I want to know why the bank took so long to forward my complaint and why they failed to give me the insurer's details so I could follow up myself.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues under DISP 1.7.1R, both of which appear to have merit on the facts as described.

**Issue 1 – Failure to provide the insurer's contact details**
DISP 1.7.1R(2) requires that when a respondent forwards a complaint, it must inform the complainant in a final response of the other respondent's contact details. The bank's letter apparently named only 'the relevant insurer' without providing a name, address, or phone number. This appears to be a clear breach of DISP 1.7.1R(2). You should confirm this by reviewing the actual forwarding letter.

**Issue 2 – Whether three weeks was 'prompt'**
DISP 1.7.1R(1) requires the respondent to forward the complaint 'promptly'. The rules do not define 'promptly' with a specific number of days, so whether three weeks meets that standard is a matter of judgement. However, given that the customer received no update for three weeks and was left without any contact details for the insurer, there is a reasonable argument that this was not prompt. You should consider whether there was any legitimate reason for the delay (e.g., complexity of identifying the correct insurer) and document that reasoning.

**Knock-on timing issue**
Under DISP 1.7.2R, once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, the standard time limits apply from the date of receipt by the insurer. Under DISP 1.6.2R, the insurer must issue a final response or an eight-week holding response within eight weeks of receiving the complaint. You should establish: (a) when exactly the insurer received the forwarded complaint, and (b) whether the eight-week clock from that date is approaching or has been missed. If the insurer has not responded and eight weeks from their receipt is imminent or past, the customer may already be entitled to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

**Recommended action**
1. Confirm the content of the forwarding letter — specifically whether contact details were omitted.
2. Identify the insurer and obtain their full contact details to provide to the customer now.
3. Establish the date the insurer received the forwarded complaint and calculate the eight-week deadline.
4. Consider whether the bank needs to acknowledge the procedural failing in its response to the customer.
5. This complaint involves a potential regulatory breach by the bank (failure to comply with DISP 1.7.1R) and a PPI-related complaint, so it should be reviewed by the Head of Customer Operations before any response is sent, in line with your internal procedure.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below assumes the bank will now provide the insurer's contact details. If those details are not yet confirmed, do not send the letter until they are. The draft does not admit liability or quantify any redress. Human review is required before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for raising these concerns with us. We are sorry for the frustration and uncertainty you have experienced.

We have reviewed the handling of your complaint about the payment protection insurance policy sold through our branch.

**Forwarding your complaint to the insurer**

When we wrote to you three weeks after receiving your complaint, we informed you that we were forwarding it to the insurer on the basis that they may be solely responsible for the matter you raised. We recognise that our letter did not include the insurer's name and contact details, and we apologise for that omission. We should have provided you with that information so that you could follow up directly if you wished to.

We can now confirm that the insurer to whom your complaint has been forwarded is:

[Insurer Name]
[Address]
[Telephone / Email]

Please quote your complaint reference number [XXXX] when contacting them.

**What happens next**

Your complaint is being treated as if it were made directly to the insurer from the date they received it from us. They are required to investigate your complaint and issue a final response within eight weeks of receiving it. If they are not in a position to do so within that timeframe, they must write to you to explain why and to confirm your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with how your complaint has been handled, or if you do not receive a satisfactory response from the insurer within the required timeframe, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet for your reference.

Once again, we apologise for the inconvenience caused by the delay in providing you with the insurer's details. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

Provides that once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, it is treated as if made directly to them from the date of receipt, meaning the eight-week clock runs from that date.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a payment protection insurance policy with your bank in 2002 and cancelled it in 2005. I recently heard from a friend that I might have been mis-sold PPI and decided to complain. Your bank has sent me a letter saying my complaint is 'time-barred' and that you won't look into whether I was mis-sold the policy. However, the letter simply states my complaint is rejected due to time limits — it doesn't explain my right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service or give me the six-month deadline for doing so. I feel this is unfair as I believe I should at least have been told about my options, even if you consider the complaint too old.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises a valid procedural point. Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but must do so in a 'final response' that complies with DISP 1.6.2R. DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response: encloses a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provides the FOS website address; informs the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied; and indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R, using the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R.

On the substance of the time bar: the PPI sale took place in 2002 and the policy was cancelled in 2005. Under DISP 2.8.9R, the FOS cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint unless the complainant referred it to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019 (or one of the limited exceptions applies). The customer has not indicated when they made their complaint — you must confirm this. If the complaint was made after 29 August 2019, the time bar under DISP 2.8.9R applies and the bank's rejection on time-bar grounds is substantively correct. However, the procedural deficiency is clear: the rejection letter did not inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS, did not enclose the FOS leaflet or provide the FOS website address, and did not include the required wording on time limits for FOS referral. This is a breach of DISP 1.6.2R read with DISP 1.8.1R.

Recommended action: Issue a remedying final response that (a) maintains the time-bar rejection if the complaint was indeed made after 29 August 2019, (b) includes all required DISP 1.6.2R content, and (c) uses the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording. You will need to decide whether to consent to waive the six-month FOS referral time limit (the customer has not yet had a compliant final response, so the six-month clock has arguably not yet started — flag this for legal/compliance review).

Assumptions to confirm before sending: (1) the date the customer's complaint was received — if it was received on or before 29 August 2019, the time bar under DISP 2.8.9R may not apply and the merits may need to be considered; (2) whether the bank wishes to consent to waive any applicable time limits; (3) whether the FOS standard explanatory leaflet is available to enclose. Human review is required given the procedural breach, the potential that the time bar may not apply depending on complaint date, and the need for a financial/legal decision on waiver of time limits.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your complaint about the payment protection insurance (PPI) policy you held with us between 2002 and 2005, and for raising your concerns about the letter we sent you.

We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly. We recognise that, even where we are unable to consider the substance of a complaint, we are required to explain your rights to you, and we should have done so. We apologise for that omission.

Our position on your complaint

Having reviewed your complaint, we are not able to consider whether your PPI policy was mis-sold. This is because complaints about the sale of PPI policies were subject to a deadline: to be considered, a complaint needed to have been referred to us or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019. As your complaint was received after that date, we are not in a position to investigate the merits of the mis-sale concern you have raised.

Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service

Even though we are unable to look into your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The Ombudsman is an independent service that can review our decision, including whether the time limits apply in your case.

You must refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service within six months of the date of this letter. If you refer it after that date, the Ombudsman may not be able to consider it.

The Ombudsman might not be able to consider your complaint if they agree that it falls outside the applicable time limits. However, that is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at: [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further information about the service and how to use it.

If you have any questions about this letter, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Permits the respondent to reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but requires the rejection to be communicated in a final response complying with DISP 1.6.2R.

Sets out the mandatory content of a final response, including enclosing the FOS leaflet, providing the FOS website address, informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicating whether the respondent consents to waive applicable time limits — none of which were included in the original rejection letter.

Provides that the FOS cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint where the complainant did not refer it to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019, subject to limited exceptions — this is the substantive time bar relied on to reject the complaint.

Sets out the general time limits for FOS referral, including the six-month period from the date of a final response, which is relevant to the customer's right to refer and the required wording in the final response.

Indicates that the six-month time limit for FOS referral is only triggered by a response that is a final response, and that the response must tell the complainant about that six-month limit — supporting the view that the original deficient letter may not have started the clock.
Prescribes the appropriate wording to be included in a final response regarding time limits for FOS referral, which must be adapted and included in the remedying final response.

claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if referred more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response. DISP 2.8.3G confirms the six-month time limit is triggered by a final response.

claims.claim: Even where we are unable to consider the substance of a complaint, we are required to explain your rights to you.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.8.1R states that even when rejecting a time-barred complaint without considering the merits, the respondent must explain this in a final response in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, which includes informing the complainant of their rights.

claims.claim: The remedying final response must include all required DISP 1.6.2R content and use the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording.

claims.claim: If the complaint was received on or before 29 August 2019, the time bar under DISP 2.8.9R may not apply and the merits may need to be considered.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(2)(a) provides that the time bar does not apply if the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019. If this condition is met, the Ombudsman can consider the complaint, and by extension the respondent cannot rely on DISP 1.8.1R to reject without considering merits.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I opened a current account with your bank online in January 2024. During the application process, I noticed that a 'Card Protection Plan' at £7.99/month was already pre-selected as part of the account setup — there was a tick already in the box. I didn't untick it because I assumed it was a required part of the account, and the page layout made it look like it was bundled in. I've now been charged £7.99 per month for the past 14 months — that's over £110 total — for something I never actively chose. When I look back at the confirmation email and all the paperwork I received when I opened the account, I also notice that at no point were you clear about how to make a complaint or that I could go to the Financial Ombudsman Service if I was unhappy. I only found out about the Ombudsman from a friend. I want a full refund of all charges for this Card Protection Plan and I want to know why I was never told about my rights to complain.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues: (1) a pre-ticked box used to enrol the customer in a Card Protection Plan (CPP) without active consent, and (2) failure to provide pre-contract information about complaint rights and the Financial Ombudsman Service.

**Issue 1 – Pre-ticked box / eligibility and suitability**
The retrieved provisions most directly applicable to payment protection contracts include ICOBS 5.1.2R, which requires a firm arranging a payment protection contract to take reasonable steps to ensure the customer only buys a policy under which they are eligible to claim benefits, and ICOBS 6.4.5R, which requires the firm to draw the customer's attention to the importance of reading documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check suitability. However, the retrieved provisions do not contain an explicit FCA rule prohibiting pre-ticked boxes or requiring active opt-in consent for add-on insurance products. The use of a pre-ticked box is a significant mis-selling concern and may engage broader FCA conduct-of-business principles, but I cannot cite a specific retrieved provision that expressly prohibits it. You should escalate this to your compliance team to confirm whether additional ICOBS or COBS provisions (not retrieved here) apply — particularly any rules on the fair presentation of optional add-ons.

**Issue 2 – Failure to provide complaint/FOS information pre-contract**
ICOBS 6.4.4AR is directly on point: it requires a firm to provide a consumer with information about (a) how to complain to the insurance undertaking and that complaints may be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service, and (b) the consumer's entitlement to compensation from the compensation scheme — and this must be provided in good time before the conclusion of the policy. The customer's allegation that this information was absent from their confirmation email and all paperwork is a potential breach of this rule. You should check the records of what was sent at account opening.

**Redress**
DISP App 3.7.2E and 3.7.3E set out the evidential approach to redress where a complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract: the firm should, as far as practicable, put the complainant in the position they would have been in had they not bought the contract, which would mean refunding all premiums paid plus simple interest. DISP App 3.7.11E also indicates the firm should consider whether to deduct the value of any paid claims. However, these are evidential provisions (not binding rules), and the question of whether the customer would have bought the CPP is a factual one requiring investigation. The customer's account of the pre-ticked box, if accurate, strongly suggests they would not have actively chosen the product.

**Assumptions and actions required before sending the customer draft:**
1. Confirm whether the CPP qualifies as a 'payment protection contract' under ICOBS — if it is purely a card protection product (covering card loss/fraud rather than loan repayments), some ICOBS provisions may not apply in the same way. This affects which rules govern the complaint.
2. Check the account-opening records to verify whether ICOBS 6.4.4A information was provided.
3. Check whether the pre-ticked box design has been reviewed by compliance previously.
4. Confirm whether any claims were made under the CPP (relevant to any deduction under DISP App 3.7.11E).
5. The customer is seeking a full refund of £111.86 (14 × £7.99). Any decision to offer redress must be made by a human reviewer — do not commit to a specific sum in the customer letter at this stage.
6. Human review is required before sending given the financial redress element, potential regulatory breach, and the assumptions above.

The draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms the investigation, addresses the FOS information point, and does not commit to a specific remedy pending investigation.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience when opening your current account, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously.

You have raised two concerns, which I will address in turn.

**The Card Protection Plan charges**

You have told us that the Card Protection Plan was pre-selected during your online account application, and that the page layout led you to believe it was a required part of the account rather than an optional add-on. You have also told us that you did not actively choose this product and have been charged £7.99 per month for 14 months as a result.

We are required, when arranging a protection policy, to take reasonable steps to ensure that you only purchase a policy under which you are eligible to claim benefits, and to draw your attention to the importance of reading the policy documentation before the end of the cancellation period so that you can check whether the policy is suitable for you. We take these obligations seriously.

We are now investigating your account-opening records to understand exactly what happened during the sign-up process and what information was presented to you at that time. We will write to you again with our findings and our decision on your request for a refund. We are not in a position to confirm the outcome of that investigation at this stage, but we want you to know that your account of events is being carefully considered.

**Information about your right to complain and the Financial Ombudsman Service**

You have told us that your confirmation email and account-opening paperwork did not make clear how to raise a complaint with us, or that you could refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We are required to provide this information to you in good time before a protection policy is concluded. We are reviewing the documentation sent to you at account opening to establish whether this information was provided as required. If it was not, we apologise unreservedly.

Regardless of what our records show, I want to make sure you have this information now: if you are not satisfied with our final response to your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

Financial Ombudsman Service
Exchange Tower
London E14 9SR
Telephone: 0800 023 4567
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk

**Next steps**

We will complete our investigation and send you a full written response as soon as possible. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Contact details]

Requires the firm to provide the consumer, in good time before conclusion of the policy, with information about how to complain and that complaints may be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service — directly engaged by the customer's allegation that this information was absent from their paperwork.

Sets out the evidential approach where a complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract: the firm should put the complainant in the position they would have been in had they not bought it, relevant to the customer's refund request.

Specifies that redress should equal the total amount paid by the complainant in respect of the payment protection contract including historic interest, less any rebate received, relevant to quantifying the potential refund.
Indicates the firm should consider whether to deduct the value of any paid claims from redress, relevant if the customer made any claims under the CPP.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan of £8,000 over the phone with your bank on 15 March. It was all done remotely — I never visited a branch. On 22 March, within the 14-day window, I called your cancellation line to exercise my right to cancel the distance contract. The adviser I spoke to said I couldn't cancel because the funds had already been disbursed and I'd started making use of the credit. She refused to process my cancellation and told me I'd need to repay the full loan with all interest over the term. I asked to speak to a manager but was told nobody was available and that I'd get a callback, which never came. I'm now being charged interest on a loan I tried to cancel within the legal timeframe. I'm 74 years old and on a fixed pension income — this additional debt is causing me significant financial distress. I also want to note that when I tried calling your complaints line to raise this issue, the number listed was an 0844 number which cost me over £3 in call charges before I got through. I want the cancellation honoured, all interest charges refunded, and compensation for the distress caused.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory issues that require urgent human review and careful handling.

**1. Right to cancel — CONC 11.1.1R and CONC 11.1.2R**
The customer took out a personal loan of £8,000 by distance contract and attempted to cancel on 22 March, within the 14-calendar-day cancellation period. Under CONC 11.1.1R, a consumer has the right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason within 14 calendar days. The adviser's refusal was almost certainly wrong: the fact that funds had been disbursed and the customer had begun using the credit does not extinguish the right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1R. You must check whether CONC 11.1.2R applies to exclude the right — specifically whether this is a regulated consumer credit agreement to which s.66A CCA applies (CONC 11.1.2R(1)(a)), or whether it is secured on land (CONC 11.1.2R(1)(b)), or falls within any other exclusion in CONC 11.1.2R. If none of those exclusions apply, the cancellation right should have been honoured.

**ASSUMPTION TO CONFIRM:** Please verify (a) whether this loan is a regulated consumer credit agreement to which s.66A CCA applies (which would exclude the CONC 11.1.1R right), and (b) whether any other CONC 11.1.2R exclusion applies. If s.66A applies, the customer may have a separate statutory withdrawal right under the CCA rather than the CONC 11 cancellation right — but that is not covered by the retrieved provisions and would require separate legal review.

**2. Consequences of valid cancellation — CONC 11.1.11R and CONC 11.1.12R**
If the cancellation was valid, the firm may only require the customer to pay for services actually provided in proportion to the contract, and that amount must not be a penalty (CONC 11.1.11R). Crucially, the firm cannot require any payment at all if it commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request (CONC 11.1.11R(2)). The firm must return any sums received from the consumer within 30 calendar days of receiving the cancellation notification (CONC 11.1.12R). Telling the customer they must repay the full loan with all interest over the term is inconsistent with these rules if the cancellation was valid.

**3. Call charges — GEN 7 (referenced in CONC 8.7.7R)**
The customer reports being charged over £3 to reach the complaints line via an 0844 number. CONC 8.7.7R directs firms to note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7. The retrieved provisions do not reproduce GEN 7 directly, so I cannot state its precise requirements from this context — flag `insufficient_context` applies to this specific point. However, this is a live regulatory concern and should be investigated separately. Do not make specific commitments about call charge refunds in the customer letter without confirming the GEN 7 position.

**4. Vulnerability**
The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and reports significant financial distress. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The response must be handled with appropriate care and the firm's vulnerability policies should be applied.

**5. Compensation for distress**
The customer requests compensation for distress. The retrieved provisions do not cover redress or distress compensation — this must be assessed by a senior handler or complaints manager under the firm's own redress framework and any applicable FCA guidance on fair outcomes.

**Recommended action:**
- Escalate immediately to a senior complaints handler given vulnerability indicators and potential regulatory breach.
- Confirm whether CONC 11.1.2R exclusions apply before sending any customer response.
- Investigate the call charges issue under GEN 7.
- Do not send the draft below without human review and confirmation of the CONC 11.1.2R position.
- Consider whether an interim goodwill gesture (e.g. suspending interest accrual pending investigation) is appropriate — but this is a business decision outside the scope of these provisions.

The draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms investigation, and does not pre-judge the outcome or make commitments on redress, given the unconfirmed assumptions above.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint very seriously.

You have told us that you entered into a personal loan agreement with us by telephone on 15 March, and that on 22 March — within 14 calendar days of the contract being made — you contacted us to exercise your right to cancel the distance contract. You have told us that our adviser refused to process your cancellation, that a promised callback from a manager did not take place, and that you have since been charged interest on the loan. You have also raised a concern about the cost of calling our complaints line.

We understand that this situation is causing you significant financial worry, and we are sorry for the distress this has caused you.

**What we are doing**

We are now investigating your complaint as a matter of priority. Our investigation will cover:

- Whether your cancellation request on 22 March was handled correctly, including whether you were entitled to cancel the distance contract within the 14-calendar-day cancellation period and whether our adviser's refusal was appropriate;
- The interest charges that have been applied to your account since your cancellation request;
- The call charges you incurred when contacting our complaints line; and
- The missed callback you were promised.

We will write to you again with our findings and our decision as soon as our investigation is complete. We aim to do this as quickly as possible given the circumstances you have described.

**Your right to refer your complaint**

If you are not satisfied with our final response, or if you have not received a final response from us within eight weeks of the date you first raised your complaint, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at:

Financial Ombudsman Service
Exchange Tower
London E14 9SR
Telephone: 0800 023 4567
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk

You would normally need to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service within six months of receiving our final response.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Date]

Sets out the exclusions from the CONC 11.1.1R cancellation right for credit agreements; must be checked to confirm whether the customer's £8,000 personal loan falls within any exclusion before the cancellation right can be confirmed or denied.
Establishes when the 14-calendar-day cancellation period begins, relevant to confirming that the customer's 22 March cancellation attempt was within the window.

Limits what the firm may charge a consumer upon valid cancellation to a proportionate amount for services actually provided, and prohibits any charge if the firm commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request.
Requires the firm to return any sums received from the consumer within 30 calendar days of receiving the cancellation notification, subject only to any permitted charge under CONC 11.1.11R.
Directs firms to note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7, relevant to the customer's complaint about the cost of calling the 0844 complaints line.

claims.claim: A consumer has the right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason within 14 calendar days.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.1R states exactly this: a consumer has a right to cancel a distance contract without penalty and without giving any reason, within 14 calendar days, where that contract is a credit agreement.

claims.claim: The fact that funds had been disbursed and the customer had begun using the credit does not extinguish the right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1R.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.1R does not condition the cancellation right on whether funds have been disbursed or used, which supports the claim implicitly. However, the provisions do not explicitly address this scenario, so the claim goes slightly beyond what is directly stated.

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.2R may exclude the right to cancel if the agreement is a regulated consumer credit agreement to which s.66A CCA applies (CONC 11.1.2R(1)(a)), or if it is secured on land (CONC 11.1.2R(1)(b)), or falls within other exclusions in CONC 11.1.2R.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.2R(1)(a) excludes regulated consumer credit agreements to which s.66A CCA applies, (1)(b) excludes agreements secured by legal mortgage on land, and other exclusions are listed in (1)(c)-(e).

claims.claim: If the cancellation was valid, the firm may only require the customer to pay for services actually provided in proportion to the contract, and that amount must not be a penalty (CONC 11.1.11R).
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R(1) states the consumer may only be required to pay for the service actually provided, the amount must not exceed a proportionate amount, and must not be construed as a penalty.

claims.claim: The firm cannot require any payment at all if it commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request (CONC 11.1.11R(2)).
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R(2) explicitly states that in no case may the firm require payment if it has commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request.

claims.claim: The firm must return any sums received from the consumer within 30 calendar days of receiving the cancellation notification (CONC 11.1.12R).
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.12R states the firm must return sums without undue delay and within 30 calendar days, beginning from the day the firm receives the notification of cancellation.

claims.claim: Telling the customer they must repay the full loan with all interest over the term is inconsistent with these rules if the cancellation was valid.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R limits what can be charged on cancellation to proportionate service costs (not a penalty), and CONC 11.1.12R requires return of sums. Requiring full loan repayment with all interest would contradict these rules.

claims.claim: CONC 8.7.7R directs firms to note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7.
claims.reasoning: CONC 8.7.7R states: 'Firms should note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7.'

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions do not reproduce GEN 7 directly, so the precise requirements of GEN 7 cannot be stated from this context.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions only reference GEN 7 but do not reproduce its content, so this is a factually accurate statement about the limitations of the retrieved material.

claims.claim: If s.66A CCA applies, the customer may have a separate statutory withdrawal right under the CCA rather than the CONC 11 cancellation right.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.3G discusses s.66A CCA (right to withdraw) and its applicability, and CONC 11.1.2R(1)(a) excludes agreements to which s.66A applies from the CONC 11.1.1R right. The claim is supported in principle but the detailed nature of the s.66A right is not fully elaborated in the retrieved provisions.

claims.claim: The cancellation period begins from the day the distance contract is made, or from the day the consumer receives the contractual terms and conditions and pre-contractual information, if later.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.5R states the cancellation period begins from the day the distance contract is made, or from the day the consumer receives the contractual terms and conditions and other pre-contractual information, if that is later.

claims.claim: The customer was entitled to exercise a right to cancel the distance contract within the 14-calendar-day cancellation period.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.1R provides a 14-calendar-day right to cancel a distance contract that is a credit agreement, subject to the exclusions in CONC 11.1.2R. The letter appropriately frames this as something being investigated rather than confirmed.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am a 72-year-old retired schoolteacher with no prior investment experience beyond a basic savings account. In January 2024, I visited my bank branch to discuss options for my £45,000 in savings. The adviser suggested I purchase a structured product linked to equity indices, describing it as 'like a savings account but with better returns.' I was not asked any questions about my investment knowledge, experience with financial instruments, or understanding of risks. The adviser simply told me to sign the application form. I have since discovered the product carries significant capital-at-risk and I do not understand how it works. I have already lost approximately £3,200 in value. I believe the bank failed to check whether this product was appropriate for someone like me, and I want my money back. I am also finding this situation very stressful as I rely on my savings for day-to-day living in retirement and am struggling financially as a result of this loss.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises serious concerns about appropriateness assessment, suitability, and potential mis-selling of a structured product to a 72-year-old retired customer with no investment experience. However, the retrieved provisions do not adequately cover the core regulatory requirements that would govern this complaint — specifically, the COBS rules on appropriateness assessment (COBS 10A), suitability (COBS 9), and the DISP complaints handling rules. The retrieved chunks relate primarily to risk summaries for high-risk investments (COBS 4 Annex 1), basic advice on stakeholder products (COBS 9.6), and pension drawdown warnings (COBS 19.10), none of which directly address the appropriateness or suitability obligations that appear to have been breached here.

The DISP App 1.4 chunk retrieved relates to endowment mortgage redress calculations and is not directly applicable to this structured product complaint.

Given the insufficient retrieved context, I cannot ground a full regulatory analysis in the provisions supplied. I am flagging this for mandatory human review for the following additional reasons:
1. The customer is 72, retired, financially dependent on her savings, and reports significant stress — there are clear indicators of potential vulnerability.
2. The complaint involves a financial loss of approximately £3,200 and a claim for full redress — a financial decision requiring human authorisation.
3. There are serious allegations of a failure to conduct any appropriateness or suitability assessment before selling a capital-at-risk structured product.
4. The description of the product as 'like a savings account but with better returns' raises potential concerns about misleading communication.
5. A regulatory deadline question arises: the sale was in January 2024 and the complaint appears recent — the handler should confirm the exact complaint receipt date and check whether any DISP response deadlines are approaching.

Recommended action: Escalate immediately to a senior complaints handler or compliance team with access to the full COBS 10A (appropriateness), COBS 9 (suitability), and DISP 1 (complaints handling) provisions. Do not send the draft customer letter below without that review. The draft below is a conservative acknowledgement only — it does not admit liability, quantify redress, or make commitments beyond acknowledging the complaint and confirming next steps.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am writing to acknowledge your complaint about the structured product you purchased in January 2024 following your visit to our branch.

I am sorry to hear that you are finding this situation stressful, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously. I understand that you are relying on your savings for day-to-day living in retirement, and I recognise how important it is that we respond to you carefully and thoroughly.

You have raised concerns that, when the product was recommended to you, you were not asked about your investment knowledge, experience, or understanding of the risks involved, and that the product was described to you in a way that did not make clear it carried a risk to your capital. You have also told us that you have experienced a loss in value of approximately £3,200.

We are now investigating your complaint fully. We will write to you again with our findings as soon as we are able to do so.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]
