structure-titan.scenario_text: I received a text message from my bank three days ago saying my complaint about a £35 overdraft fee had been resolved after I agreed on the phone to accept a refund. However, the written summary I then received by post only said 'your complaint is now closed' — it didn't mention anything about the Financial Ombudsman Service, didn't give me their website address, and didn't tell me I could refer the matter to them if I changed my mind. Well, I have changed my mind. The £35 refund doesn't address the fact that the fee was applied incorrectly in the first place due to a system error, and I believe I'm owed compensation for the stress and time spent chasing this. I want to escalate this complaint. I also want to know why the bank failed to give me proper information about my rights when it sent me that summary letter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have come in via the handler's inbox but reads as if it is written from the customer's perspective. I will treat it as the handler relaying the customer's position and draft accordingly — please confirm if that is correct before sending anything.

Regulatory position:

1. The written summary sent to the customer ('your complaint is now closed') appears to have been intended as a summary resolution communication (SRC) following a telephone agreement to accept a refund. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the firm's own procedure at section 7.5), any communication that closes a complaint — whether a final response or an SRC — must inform the customer of their right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, including the FOS contact details and the six-month time limit for doing so. The summary letter as described did none of this. This is a procedural failing that needs to be acknowledged.

2. Because the six-month FOS referral window is only triggered by a response that properly notifies the customer of that right (DISP 2.8.3G), the customer's time to refer to the FOS has arguably not yet started to run. You should not, however, state this as a certainty in the customer letter — it is a legal/regulatory interpretation that should be confirmed by your compliance team or legal counsel before you commit to it in writing.

3. The customer is now raising two distinct issues: (a) the adequacy of the £35 refund given the underlying system error, and (b) a claim for compensation for stress and time. The complaint has therefore been re-opened or extended. This is a new or continuing complaint that requires a proper final response under DISP 1.6.2R, including a clear outcome, reasons, and full FOS referral rights.

4. The complaint involves a potential financial redress decision (compensation beyond the £35 already agreed) and a regulatory compliance failing (the deficient summary letter). Both factors require human review before any response is sent.

5. The internal procedure requires complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or regulatory significance, to be countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations. This complaint has potential regulatory significance (the deficient SRC), so that review requirement applies even if the monetary value is below £500.

Recommended action:
- Do not send the draft below without human review and sign-off.
- Refer to compliance/legal to confirm whether the six-month FOS clock has started.
- Investigate the system error allegation and assess whether additional redress beyond the £35 is warranted.
- Issue a proper final response (not another bare summary) once the investigation is complete, with full FOS referral wording.
- Assumption flagged: the draft below does not commit to a specific outcome on compensation, as that has not been assessed. It also does not state whether the FOS clock has or has not started — it simply confirms the customer's right to refer. Adjust once the compliance position is confirmed.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting back to us. I am sorry to hear that you are not satisfied with how your complaint has been handled, and I want to address both the concerns you have raised.

First, regarding the written summary you received: we acknowledge that the letter you received did not include information about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We are required to include that information whenever we close a complaint, and we fell short of that standard. I apologise for this.

Second, you have raised additional concerns — specifically, that the £35 refund does not fully address the impact of the system error that caused the fee to be applied incorrectly, and that you have experienced stress and spent time chasing this matter. We take these concerns seriously. We are treating your complaint as ongoing and will investigate these points fully.

We will write to you again with our findings and our final response. That letter will set out clearly what we have concluded and what, if anything, we are offering to put things right.

In the meantime, we want to make sure you are aware of your rights. If you are dissatisfied with our final response, or if we have not issued one within eight weeks of the date we first received your complaint, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

Once you receive our final response, you will have six months from the date of that letter to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remain dissatisfied.

Thank you for your patience. We will be in touch as soon as our investigation is complete.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with how my complaint was handled. Three weeks ago, I contacted your bank about an incorrect fee of £45 charged to my current account. Your staff member called me back the same day and offered to refund the fee, which I accepted over the phone. I then received a short letter saying the complaint was resolved, but the letter did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service at all — no website address, no information about my right to refer the matter if I changed my mind, nothing. I have since looked into this further and I believe the fee was actually part of a wider pattern of overcharging on my account going back several months. I am no longer satisfied with the resolution and wish to escalate this matter. I want the bank to investigate the full pattern of charges and I want to know why I was not properly informed of my rights when the complaint was first closed.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that need to be addressed separately.

**Issue 1: Missing FOS referral information in the summary resolution communication**
The original complaint appears to have been resolved under the 'next business day rule' (DISP 1.5, referenced in the internal procedure) — the staff member called back the same day and the customer accepted a refund. However, the short letter sent to the customer did not include FOS referral rights. Under Crest Bank's procedure (which references DISP 1.6.2R), all communications closing a complaint must inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS, including the FOS contact details (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; 0800 023 4567) and the six-month time limit from the date of the final response. The omission of this information from the closing letter is a procedural failing that must be acknowledged.

Importantly, under DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month FOS referral window is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response, redress determination, or summary resolution communication — and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit. Because the original letter did not include this information, it is arguable that the six-month clock has not yet started running. You should confirm with your compliance team whether the original letter constitutes a valid summary resolution communication given the omission, and whether a corrective letter is needed to properly start the clock. This is a material uncertainty.

**Issue 2: New/escalated complaint about a wider pattern of overcharging**
The customer is now raising a new and broader complaint — a pattern of alleged overcharging going back several months. This is a substantively different complaint from the original £45 fee dispute and must be logged as a new complaint in the CMS, with a new complaint reference number, in accordance with the internal procedure. It will need to be investigated impartially, and a final response must be issued within eight weeks of receipt. Given the complexity (multiple charges, multiple months), consider whether escalation to the Head of Customer Operations is required under section 7.3 of the procedure.

**Recommended action:**
1. Log the new/escalated complaint immediately and assign a reference number.
2. Send the customer a written acknowledgement within five business days confirming the reference number, handler contact details, and expected resolution timescale.
3. Draft a response that: (a) acknowledges the procedural failing in the original closing letter; (b) confirms the customer's FOS referral rights (which should have been included originally); and (c) confirms the new complaint about the wider pattern of charges is being investigated.
4. Flag to compliance whether the original closing letter needs to be remedied to properly trigger the six-month FOS referral window.
5. **Human review is required** before sending — this involves a potential financial redress element (multiple charges), a regulatory procedural failing, and uncertainty about whether the original summary resolution communication was valid.

Note: The customer draft below assumes the new complaint is being accepted and investigated, and that the bank is acknowledging the omission. It does not commit to any specific financial outcome on the wider charges investigation. The FOS referral rights notice is included now to remedy the earlier omission, but you must confirm with compliance whether a separate remedying letter is also needed in respect of the original closing letter.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for taking the time to explain your concerns. I am sorry that your experience has fallen short of the standard you should expect from us.

Your complaint raises two matters, and I want to address both clearly.

**Your original complaint and the closing letter**

When you contacted us three weeks ago about the £45 fee, we arranged a same-day call-back and offered to refund the charge, which you accepted. We then sent you a letter confirming the matter was resolved. I am sorry to tell you that this letter should have included information about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remained dissatisfied, and it did not. This was an error on our part, and I apologise for it.

To put this right: you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, if you are not satisfied with how we have handled it. Their contact details are:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

You have six months from the date of our final response to refer your complaint to them. Please note that because the required information was not included in our earlier letter, you should contact us or the Financial Ombudsman Service if you have any questions about the applicable time limit.

**Your new concern about a wider pattern of charges**

You have told us that you now believe the £45 fee may be part of a broader pattern of overcharging on your account going back several months. We take this seriously. We are treating this as a new complaint and will investigate it fully and impartially, reviewing the relevant account records and transaction history.

Your complaint reference number for this investigation is: **[REFERENCE NUMBER]**

Your case is being handled by [Handler Name], who can be contacted at [contact details]. We will aim to provide you with a full response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to remind you of your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

Thank you again for raising this with us. We will be in touch with our findings.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £340 applied to my current account between June and September this year. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating the charges were applied correctly under the account terms and conditions. However, the letter did not include any information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, no leaflet was enclosed, no website address was provided, and there was no mention of any time limits for me to refer my complaint elsewhere. I feel I have been left completely in the dark about my options. I believe the charges were unfair as I was never notified that my account had gone overdrawn before the charges were applied, and I want a full refund. I also want to know what my rights are now, since your so-called 'final response' told me nothing about where I can escalate this matter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that must be addressed separately.

**Issue 1: Defective final response letter (procedural — clear regulatory breach)**
The final response letter dated 15 November is defective under DISP 1.6.2R. That rule requires a final response to: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R regarding the six-month time limit. None of these were included. This is a clear breach of DISP 1.6.2R. A remedying letter must be sent to the customer as a matter of urgency, enclosing the FOS leaflet, providing the FOS contact details, and including the prescribed Annex 3R wording. Note: the six-month referral window under DISP 2.8.2R(1) is only triggered by a valid final response (see DISP 2.8.3G), so the clock has arguably not yet started running — but you must confirm this with your compliance team before relying on it in correspondence. The internal procedure (section 7.5 of the Customer Complaints Handling Procedure) also requires FOS details to be included in all final response letters.

**Issue 2: Substantive complaint — unarranged overdraft charges (£340, June–September)**
The customer alleges the charges were unfair because they received no prior notification that the account had gone overdrawn. The retrieved provisions (CONC 5C.2.1R, CONC 5C.3.2R) address the structure and enforceability of overdraft charges — specifically that charges must be expressed as an annual interest rate and that unarranged overdraft charges imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b) are unenforceable and recoverable. However, the customer's specific allegation is about lack of prior notification before charges were applied, and the retrieved provisions do not directly address a notification obligation. You will need to investigate: (1) whether the charges were structured as required by CONC 5C.2.1R (i.e. expressed as an annual interest rate, not a flat fee); (2) whether the unarranged overdraft rate exceeded the arranged overdraft rate in breach of CONC 5C.3.1R; and (3) what the account terms say about notification. The substantive complaint cannot be resolved on the current information.

**Recommended action:**
1. Issue a remedying letter immediately to correct the procedural defect in the 15 November response — this is the customer draft below.
2. Separately, investigate the substantive charge complaint before issuing a revised final response on the merits.
3. Because this involves potential financial redress (£340) and a regulatory breach, this must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch (per internal procedure section 7.4).
4. Flag for human review — see flags below.

**Assumptions to confirm:** (a) The 15 November letter is the only response sent — no separate FOS notification was issued. (b) The complaint was received within the last eight weeks (or if more than eight weeks have passed, a separate eight-week written response obligation under DISP 1.6.2R(2) may also have been triggered). (c) The charges were flat fees or structured charges rather than annual interest rates — this needs verification against the account terms to assess CONC 5C.2.1R compliance. (d) The prescribed Annex 3R wording to use: assuming the bank does not consent to waive the six-month time limit, wording option (1) from DISP 1 Annex 3R applies — this is reflected in the draft below, but confirm the bank's position on waiver before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry that our letter of 15 November did not include important information about your options if you remain dissatisfied with our response. We recognise this was an error on our part, and we want to put that right now.

**Your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you are not satisfied with our response to your complaint about the overdraft charges applied to your account, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge – but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

If you do not refer your complaint in time, the Ombudsman will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances. For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at:
- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which sets out further information about the service and how to use it.

**Your substantive complaint**

We also want to let you know that we are reviewing your complaint about the overdraft charges of £340 applied to your account between June and September. We will write to you separately with our findings once that review is complete.

We are sorry for any confusion caused by our earlier letter. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

Prescribes the wording to be included in a final response where the respondent does not consent to waive the six-month time limit in DISP 2.8.2R(1) — this wording has been incorporated into the customer draft.

Sets the six-month time limit within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response, and the broader time limits the bank must address in its prescribed wording.

Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a valid final response that tells the complainant about the time limit — relevant to assessing whether the clock has started running given the defective 15 November letter.

Indicates that FOS information required in responses under the complaints time limit rules should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of the response.

Requires overdraft charges to be expressed as an annual interest rate — relevant to assessing whether the £340 charges were structured lawfully, which must be investigated as part of the substantive complaint.

Provides that unarranged overdraft charges imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b) are unenforceable and recoverable — relevant to the potential redress position if the charges are found to have been unlawfully structured.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the final response I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised transactions on my current account. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating that I had failed to keep my PIN secure. However, I believe this conclusion is unfair as I have never shared my PIN with anyone and I reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. Your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It also failed to tell me whether you consent to waive any time limits if I refer my complaint to the Ombudsman. I feel I have been treated unfairly and I want to know what my options are now. I am a pensioner on a fixed income and the loss of £1,200 from these transactions has left me unable to pay my heating bills this winter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This is a complaint about a rejected complaint response that was procedurally deficient under DISP 1.6.2R. The customer's original complaint concerned unauthorised transactions on a current account (£1,200 loss). The bank issued a final response on 15 November rejecting the complaint, but that final response was non-compliant in three respects under DISP 1.6.2R: (1) it did not enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (2) it did not provide the FOS website address; and (3) it did not indicate whether the bank consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R (the DISP 1 Annex 3R wording). These are binding requirements under DISP 1.6.2R, not guidance.

The bank must now issue a remedying letter that corrects all three omissions. This letter should: enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provide the FOS website address (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk); and include the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time-limit waiver consent. The customer must also be told they may now refer the complaint to the FOS if they remain dissatisfied.

IMPORTANT ASSUMPTIONS AND FLAGS FOR HUMAN REVIEW:
1. VULNERABILITY: The customer has explicitly stated they are a pensioner on a fixed income and that the £1,200 loss has left them unable to pay heating bills. This is a clear indicator of financial vulnerability and potential hardship. This must be considered in how the bank handles the matter going forward.
2. FINANCIAL REDRESS: The complaint involves a £1,200 loss from alleged unauthorised transactions. The substantive merits of the rejection (the PIN security allegation) are not addressed in this draft — that is a separate question requiring investigation. This draft only remedies the procedural deficiencies in the final response letter. The handler must confirm whether the substantive rejection decision has been properly reviewed, particularly in light of the customer's vulnerability.
3. REDRESS THRESHOLD: Under the internal policy (section 7.4), complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. At £1,200, this letter requires that sign-off.
4. ASSUMPTION ON FINAL RESPONSE STATUS: The draft below treats the new letter as a remedying communication that corrects the deficient final response and re-states the FOS referral right. I am assuming the bank is not reopening or changing the substantive rejection decision at this stage — only remedying the procedural omissions. If the bank intends to reconsider the merits, the letter will need to be substantially revised.
5. TIME LIMITS: The six-month period for the customer to refer to the FOS runs from the date of the final response. The original final response was dated 15 November. The remedying letter will reset or clarify that clock — confirm the exact date of the original letter and take legal/compliance advice on whether the six-month period runs from 15 November or from the date of this remedying letter.
6. The substantive allegation (unauthorised transactions, PIN security) may engage BCOBS 5 and other conduct obligations not covered by the retrieved provisions. Human review is required before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry that our final response letter dated 15 November did not contain all the information it should have, and I apologise for any additional distress this has caused you.

We have reviewed our letter and we recognise that it did not include certain important information that you were entitled to receive. Specifically, it did not enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, did not provide their website address, and did not tell you whether we consent to waive any relevant time limits should you wish to refer your complaint to the Ombudsman. We are sorry for these omissions and are writing now to put that right.

Our position on the substance of your complaint remains as set out in our letter of 15 November. We have rejected your complaint on the basis set out in that letter. We understand you disagree with this conclusion, and we take your concerns seriously.

Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The Financial Ombudsman Service is an independent service that resolves disputes between financial businesses and their customers.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at:
Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.

[HANDLER TO INSERT DISP 1 ANNEX 3R PRESCRIBED WORDING ON TIME-LIMIT WAIVER CONSENT HERE — this must be confirmed by compliance before despatch]

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

Indicates that information about the FOS required in responses under the complaints time limit rules should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of the response.
Referenced in DISP 1.6.2R as one of the time limits in respect of which the bank must indicate whether it consents to waive — this indication was absent from the 15 November letter.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with your bank's handling of my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £487 applied to my current account in March 2024. I originally submitted my complaint on 15 April 2024. I received your final response letter dated 28 May 2024 which rejected my complaint, stating the charges were correctly applied under the account terms and conditions. However, I believe your response was inadequate and unfair. You failed to properly consider that I was in hospital at the time the charges accrued and had no ability to manage my account. Furthermore, I note that your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It simply said I could 'contact the ombudsman if unhappy' without any further detail about the time limits for doing so. I want to know what my rights are and whether I can still escalate this matter, as it has now been approximately five months since I received your response.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a handler. I am treating it as a complaint escalation for the handler to action.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Defective final response — FOS referral rights:** The customer's final response letter dated 28 May 2024 appears to have been non-compliant with DISP 1.6.2R. That rule requires the final response to: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R regarding whether the bank consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. The customer states none of (a), (b), or (d) were included, and (c) was only partially addressed. This is a material deficiency.

2. **Six-month FOS time limit — triggered or not?** Under DISP 2.8.2R, the complainant ordinarily has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. However, DISP 2.8.3G makes clear that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that is a valid final response — and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit. If the 28 May 2024 letter did not include the required FOS information (including the time limit), there is a strong argument that it did not constitute a compliant final response capable of starting the six-month clock. This is a legal/regulatory judgement that must be confirmed by a senior reviewer or legal team before communicating to the customer.

3. **Potential exceptional circumstances — hospitalisation:** The customer states they were hospitalised when the charges accrued. Under DISP 2.8.4G, incapacitation is cited as an example of exceptional circumstances that could allow the FOS to consider a complaint outside the normal time limits. This is guidance, not a rule, and the FOS makes its own determination. However, it is a relevant factor. This also raises potential vulnerability considerations.

4. **Overdraft charges substance:** The complaint concerns unarranged overdraft charges of £487 in March 2024. CONC 5C.2.1R and CONC 5C.3 (unarranged overdraft interest rate cap) may be relevant to whether the charges were correctly applied, but the retrieved provisions do not give sufficient detail to assess the substantive merits of the rejection. The handler should review the original investigation file.

5. **Internal policy:** Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.5) requires FOS contact details (website and telephone) and the six-month time limit to be included in all final response letters. The 28 May letter appears to have breached this internal requirement.

**Recommended action:**
- Do not send any customer response without senior/legal review given: (i) the defective final response issue; (ii) the unresolved question of whether the six-month clock has started; (iii) the vulnerability indicator (hospitalisation); and (iv) financial redress below £500 but close to the threshold requiring Head of Customer Operations sign-off.
- Retrieve and review the original 28 May 2024 final response letter to confirm exactly what FOS information was or was not included.
- Consider whether a remedying letter is needed that properly complies with DISP 1.6.2R — if so, that letter would need to include the FOS leaflet, website address, and the DISP 1 Annex 3R time-limit waiver wording, and would restart the six-month clock from its date of issue.
- The substantive rejection of the overdraft charges complaint should also be reviewed in light of the hospitalisation circumstances and the CONC 5C provisions.
- Flag vulnerability: the customer was hospitalised during the relevant period.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending customer draft:**
- Confirm the exact contents of the 28 May 2024 letter (was the FOS leaflet enclosed? Was the website address given? Was the Annex 3R wording included?).
- Confirm whether the bank wishes to issue a remedying compliant final response or to treat the original as valid.
- Confirm whether the substantive decision to reject is being maintained or reconsidered.
- Legal/compliance input is needed on whether the defective letter means the six-month clock has not yet started.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied following our response of 28 May 2024, and we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at the time the charges were applied.

We take our obligations to customers very seriously, and we want to make sure you are fully informed of your rights.

Having reviewed your correspondence, we are concerned that our letter of 28 May 2024 may not have provided you with complete information about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). We are looking into this as a matter of urgency.

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, if you remain dissatisfied with our response. The FOS can be contacted as follows:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

We are enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, which sets out how the FOS works and the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.

We are also reviewing the concerns you have raised about our original response, including the circumstances you have described. We will write to you again shortly with a further update.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Contact details]

Sets binding requirements on how overdraft charges must be structured as interest rates — potentially relevant to whether the charges of £487 were correctly applied, though substantive assessment requires further review.
